sections.title: Premise
sections.body:
Canonical lore for the ten-chapter arc. Chapter One ("Emberwake", `public/js/chapter1.js`) is
fixed; everything below exists to explain it, not to revise it. The Lanternstead chapter is
likewise shipped content (now `public/js/chapter3.js`, in-engine Chapter Three — see §5,
Ch. 3; Dellhollow is `chapter2.js`). When writing dialogue, prefer the plain-knowledge
register in §7 — villagers
talk about lamps the way sailors talk about tide.

**Premise in one breath:** Most of the world is ordinary flameless towns living on plain human
warmth — perfectly fine, and always has been. But in a handful of far valleys — the "fire
villages" most people have heard of and never seen — people keep a communal flame, and what
they give it is the *heart* of them: joys and griefs told once and given over, which the flame
radiates back through the street lamps as one shared warmth. One family per village is paid the
opposite duty: to keep the flame, and to carry their own hearts the hard way. When Emberbrook's
flame — one of the last alive — is carried off in a single heartbeat, leaving a village that
knows every fact of itself and feels none of them, the only two people whose names still feel
like their own — a lamplighter who never swore his vow, and a mapmaker who has spent eleven
years searching for a hometown she cannot name — must carry the last live spark back to the
fire it came from.

**The lore budget (writers' rule):** this game carries exactly ONE deep fictional system — the
flame institution and its appendages: the moths, the Warden, the shifting wood. Everything else
— every place, culture, and character — must run on familiar, relatable human themes: colorful
surfaces, instantly-understood motivations. Locks and river eels, guild politics, a mother and
a daughter, a man and his grief. No new invented systems, ever, without explicit director
sign-off. If a chapter needs a second magic to work, the chapter is wrong.

sections.title: 1. The Kindling and the Order of Lamplighters (deep background)
sections.body:
Deep in the Whisperwood burns the **Kindling**: the first fire, older than any village, burning
in a stone bowl in a clearing. It is a fire that *keeps* — not the things told to it, but what
they weighed. Tell it a grief and the grief is truly given over; tell it a joy and the joy is
banked. The fire holds the felt residue of every telling, forever, and warms everyone in reach
of its light with all of it. People have always brought it their dead, their weddings, their
bad winters.

The **Order of Lamplighters** grew up around it: walkers who carried seed-embers of the Kindling
out of the wood and lit a **Heartlight** in each valley — a daughter-fire with the same keeping
nature, seated in crystal on a stone pedestal so it could burn without fuel. The Order's whole
creed is one sentence: ***light does not die — it is only ever carried.*** Fire from the Kindling
is never "made" and never "put out"; it is passed, hand to hand, wick to wick.

The Order always walked in **pairs**: a **Flamebearer**, who carried the seed-ember, and a
**Waykeeper**, who carried the roads in their head — because the Whisperwood does not hold still,
and no drawn map of it survives a season. *"The map does not know fire. The flame does not know
the way."* (Rowan quotes this in Chapter One without knowing he's quoting.) Every gate the Order
built — including Emberbrook's Old Gate — is cut with **twin sigils** that open only for two
people standing them together, because that road was never meant to be walked alone.

Three hundred years ago the Kindling began to fail — burning low, blue, cold. The Order's
walkers were recalled to tend it. Most refused: they had villages now, neighbors, children. They
sealed the gates from the valley side (standard protocol against the wood's moths), stayed on as
resident lamplighter families, and the annual circuit — the re-lighting walk from the Kindling to
every Heartlight — simply stopped. One officer stayed with the mother-fire: the **Warden of the
Kindling**. He is still there. He is the Stranger with the pale-blue lantern.

**The rare flames.** The Order seeded Heartlights widely, in its day — valley after valley, a
whole geography of daughter-fires. But a Heartlight lives by unbroken keeping (§2), and once
the circuit died, the keepers' lines began to fail: a keeper died vowless, a family moved on, a
village emptied. For three centuries the Warden has been recalling the orphaned and failed
lights, one by one (§4), and he is nearly done. **Only a handful of living Heartlights remain.**
Most of the world is ordinary flameless towns living on plain human warmth — perfectly fine,
per the felt-model in §3. Flame-villages are remote, close-knit, unusually joyful, and
half-legendary: "fire villages" most people have heard of and never seen. Emberbrook survived
on two accidents — an unbroken keeper line and a sealed gate. Consequences writers must hold:
**Vesper, a seasoned traveler, had plausibly never seen a living Heartlight before Chapter
One**, and the wider world does not well understand Heartlights at all. (Rowan's Chapter One
line, "every Heartlight in every valley is a child of that fire," is the old formula — true of
origins, spoken by a man who has never left his valley and has no idea how few are left.)

sections.title: 2. The Lamplighter Tradition — what Lake's job actually is

subs.title: The Heartlight
subs.body:
Emberbrook's Heartlight is a seed of the Kindling, carried into the valley three hundred years
ago and lit on the pedestal in the square the day the village was founded. Like its mother, it
keeps what it is told — not the moments, the *meaning* of them. Three hundred years of feeling
live in it: the joy of every wedding, the trust remade after every argument, every good loaf and
bad winter given over and given back. It is not an archive; nothing can be looked up in it. It
is the village's heart, held in common — and it **radiates**. Stand in its light and you stand
inside three centuries of your neighbors' best and worst days, resolved into one warmth. The
power of this is enormous in the quietest way: camaraderie, shared identity, wisdom that stays
learned (Emberbrook does not repeat its old mistakes, because the understanding that ended them
is alive in the light), motivation, low strife. An individual's gift becomes the community's
anchor.

By design, there is nothing to snoop: a telling dissolves into the radiance anonymously, like a
cup of water into a pond. The flame gives back warmth, never moments. Nobody — not the teller,
not a neighbor, not the flame itself — can fish a particular evening back out.

subs.title: The lamps
subs.body:
The street lamps are not decoration and not (only) illumination. The Heartlight is the
reservoir; the lamps are how the warmth reaches you. Each lamp is lit *from* the Heartlight's
line of fire, and while a lamp burns it does two concrete things:

1. **It carries the radiance.** The shared warmth travels the flame, and the flame lives in the
   lamps: a lit lamp bathes its street in everything the village has ever given over. Streets
   with dead lamps go *dull* — out of the light, the communal warmth fades for you: neighbors
   feel a little more like strangers, mornings start slow and pointless, old quarrels itch.
   Everyone keeps every memory they ever had — they just stop being warmed by anyone else's.
   Villagers know this the way they know damp gives you a cough. "Dark lamp, dull street" is
   not a proverb; it is a mechanism.
2. **It wards off moths.** Moths (see §3) nestle into unwarded light and sleeping minds and fray
   what they touch. A properly lit lamp is a closed door to them.

subs.title: The rounds
subs.body:
Every dusk, the lamplighter takes the brass lighter down from above the hearth and walks the
circuit in fixed order — low ground first (the pond lane, where moths rise off the water at
dusk), then inward, ending at the lamps nearest the Heartlight: *closing the ring* before full
dark. This is the job, stated plainly: **carry the flame from the village's heart to a lamppost
near every home, so that nobody sleeps outside the warmth.** At each lamp he opens the little
brass door, touches flame to wick, **and means it**.

"Meaning it" is not poetry — under this model it is obvious: a lamp's whole work is to carry
feeling, so it must be lit *with* feeling. Any fire will light a wick; only a *keeper's* fire,
given with a moment of genuine attention — a real thought, a real feeling of the day, spent on
that lamp — makes it a carrying lamp. A lamp lit without meaning gutters by midnight: there is
nothing in it to carry. This is the lamplighter's private, nightly version of the telling:
instead of giving his heart to the Heartlight, he spends it in small coin into the lamps and the
lighter, every night of his life. Grandmother never explained "what meaning it involves" because
it can't be explained, only paid.

If a lamp goes dark overnight, nothing dramatic happens — which is exactly why the duty needs
ritual discipline. One dark night is a dull morning. A dark season is a street that slowly cools
toward itself — flatter greetings, doors shut a little earlier. The Hush is the instantaneous,
total version of an ill every villager already knows in miniature.

subs.title: Why one family
subs.body:
The seed-flame stays lit through **unbroken keeping**: it must always be *claimed* — carried by
someone who tends it and means to pass it on. Hand-to-hand succession inside one household is
how the claim never lapses. The office also carries a real cost that can't be casually shared
(see §3: lamplighters are forbidden the telling — they carry their own hearts whole), so it
concentrates in a family that raises its children to bear it. Lake's line has held Emberbrook's
lighter since the gate closed — an unbroken keeping which is, together with the sealed gate,
the whole reason Emberbrook still burns when nearly every other Heartlight has been recalled
(§1).

subs.title: The brass lighter
subs.body:
The lighter is not a tool for making fire; it is a **seed-ember of the Kindling itself** — the
same flame that lit the Heartlight, kept small and portable, the Order's original walking-fire.
It burns no fuel. It never goes out because "out" is not something that happens to carried
light: it stays lit as long as it is *kept* — claimed, tended, meant. It would die only if its
keeper deliberately quenched it, or if it were abandoned with no one taking it up. Grandmother's
"it wasn't hers to put out" is Order law stated as kitchen wisdom: a keeper carries the flame;
the flame belongs to the Kindling. (This is the gun on the mantel for Chapter Ten.)

The lighter is a **dual vessel**, and the second half of that is a secret even from Lake. As a
seed of the Kindling on the Heartlight's line, it is a *walking piece of the communal warmth* —
the one hearth in the valley that travels: wherever the lamplighter carries it, the village's
radiance goes with him. And because the nightly "meaning it" spends small pieces of the
lamplighter's *own* feeling into the brass, night after night for a lifetime, it is also the one
vault of **Lake's own heart**. He stands outside the communal telling — and has been unknowingly
banking himself into the lighter his whole life.

Stated plainly, because the arc leans on it: **ordinary villagers cannot wield this fire.** An
unmeant flame gutters by midnight; carrying takes the consecrated nightly practice, paid for
years. And a villager who strays beyond the lamplight dims — their feeling lives in the flame,
and only a practiced keeper can carry a seed of it out to them. The lamplighter is not a man
with a torch; he is the only person in the valley the warmth will travel with.

Lake took the lighter up the night his grandmother died and has carried it every dusk since. He never swore
the Keeper's vow — but *carrying is claiming*, and that unbroken year of rounds is why the
lighter, alone in the valley, was not takeable on Emberwake.

subs.title: Emberwake and the Kindling Hour
subs.body:
Emberwake, the last night of autumn, is the anniversary of the founding fire — the "wake of the
ember" in both senses: the trail of the carried ember that founded the village, and a vigil for
the year now ending. In the old days it was the night the circuit-walkers arrived from the
Kindling to renew the Heartlight. The walkers stopped coming three centuries ago; the festival
stayed. The **Kindling Hour** is the one hour of the year the Heartlight *opens* — reaches back
along its old line toward the mother-fire — and the whole village queues up to make the year's
tellings: "bring a memory worth keeping." You tell the flame your year; you keep the memory; the
flame takes what it weighed, and everyone is warmer for it. Nothing starts until the lamps are
lit, because you do not open the village's heart with the ring unwarded. Hence Poppy: Lake is
the only soul allowed to be late tonight.

sections.title: 3. Feeling and Flame — the metaphysics, stated plainly
sections.body:
**Ordinary memory is untouched by any of this.** Everyone in the valley keeps their own factual
memories, in their own heads, everywhere, always — same as a traveler, same as a flameless town.
What a keeping flame takes, holds, and gives back is something else: the **felt residue** of
experience. Joys. Griefs given over. The trust rebuilt after a quarrel. Love of a place and its
people, commitments, understandings, hard-won wisdom. Not a ledger of moments — a shared heart.

**The telling** is a gift in one direction and a radiance in the other. You tell the flame a
memory; you keep the memory; the flame takes what it *meant* — and shines that meaning back out
on everyone in the light, forever. One villager's banked joy becomes the whole street's warmth;
one grief, given over, stops being a private stone and becomes something the whole village
quietly carries together. This is why the tradition exists and why it is worth a family's whole
line to tend it: villages with a Heartlight are *knit*. They hold together, they do not repeat
their old mistakes (the understanding that ended a feud stays alive in the light long after the
feud's details bore everyone), and they get up in the morning knowing why.

**Proximity is the whole game.** The radiance reaches you through fire on the Heartlight's line
— the lamps. Inside the ring of light: anchored. Outside it: living on your own heart. Travelers
and flameless towns are perfectly fine — a little lonelier, running on what they carry, the way
everyone did before the Kindling was found. And that is *most of the world*: the flameless town
is the ordinary case, not the exception, and it holds together on plain human warmth — family,
habit, neighborliness, the usual glue. (Dellhollow, Ch. 2, is the on-screen proof, and the
quiet challenge it hands Lake.) But a *villager* who has spent a lifetime giving feeling over
has banked most of their heart, and dims when they stray beyond the lamplight — which is why
nobody wanders far, and why only a practiced keeper, carrying a meant seed, can bring the
warmth out to them (§2, the lighter). "Dark lamp, dull street" is literal.

**Anonymity is built in.** A feeling dissolves into the flame like water into water. Nothing can
be browsed, revisited, or traced to its teller. The flame has no index, keeps no moments, and
answers no questions. It only warms.

**Names.** A child's name is the **first telling**: every Emberbrook baby is named aloud to the
Heartlight. What the flame takes is not the word — it is the *bond*: the belonging of that name
to that child, and of that child to the village. A name told to the flame sits in the communal
warmth for life; villagers feel *addressed* by the light. This is why the Hush's signature
injury is a villager holding their own name like a coin found in the road: they can say the
word; it doesn't feel like theirs.

**The Hush, precisely.** The Hush is not amnesia; it is *unmooring*. When the flame left, every
fact stayed and every mooring went. Villagers keep their skills, their histories, the words of
their own names — and lose the feelings, motivations, commitments, and connections those facts
were anchored to. They are not blank; they are *unattached*: lost, unmotivated, disconnected
from the people around them, reading their own lives like paperwork.

**Who stood outside the Hush on Emberwake, and why:**
- **Vesper** is untouched because none of her feeling was ever in this flame. She arrived an
  hour ago and told Emberbrook's flame nothing; her heart is her own, same as any traveler's.
  (Her deeper connection to the *Kindling* is separate — §4.)
- **Lake** is untouched, for two stacked reasons. First, Order law: **the keeper must stand
  outside the kept** — a lamplighter is forbidden the telling and carries his own heart whole,
  because someone must still *feel the duty* on the day the flame fails. Nothing of Lake was
  ever in the Heartlight. Second, his trade's small nightly spendings live in the **lighter**,
  which is claimed and kept and therefore untouchable — "My lighter's still warm... Not this
  one." Both facts are true; Lake only knows the second one yet.
- **Rowan** half-resists: forty years of the ledger live in his hands and habits — a commitment
  worn so deep into the body that "I keep the ledger" still feels true when nothing else does.
  But his name was told at his naming, and the bond of it went with the rest: he knows the word
  and cannot make it his.
- **The villagers** keep their bodies' knowledge and their heads' facts, and lose *why any of it
  mattered, and to whom*. They still walk, bake, tie knots — they've lost being someone doing
  it, with someone, for someone.
- **Mochi** keeps everything, improved. Cats never tell anything to anyone; a cat's whole self —
  memory and heart alike — lives in the body, "where moths can't reach." Every lamplighter
  household keeps a cat for exactly this: cats see moths, hunt moths, and keep them off
  sleepers. Grandmother's saying is professional literature.

**Rowan's ledger** is the counterexample that proves the model: ink keeps *facts*, and facts
survive. Every page is exactly as legible the morning after the Hush as the morning before —
that is the tragedy of it. A book full of true things nobody feels: weddings that read like
weather reports, a flood that reads like an invoice. Ink is a rack to hang feeling on, and the
feelings have left the building. (Vesper's post-Hush notebook matters for the mirrored reason —
§4.)

**Moths** are stray, unanchored feeling. When a keeping flame dies or is broken open, its warmth
does not vanish — it scatters, and each stray feeling takes wing as a pale moth. A moth is
homeless and hungry: it seeks warmth to stay aloft, nestling into unwarded light and sleeping
minds and fraying what it touches — so the folk belief that "moths eat light" and the truth that
"moths *are* light" are the same fact. On the road beyond the gate: "That's where the light
went. Some of it never made it to the sky." Stray moths can be gathered home by a keeper's meant
flame — this becomes a core mechanic and a Chapter Seven turn.

**Animals feel keeping-fire the way they feel weather.** The fish circling the pond all day were
feeling the Heartlight's line strain as the recall gathered; they stopped the moment it snapped.
Mochi has been watching the north road all evening for the same reason. (The Tenant, Ch. 2,
came up from a lower river whose banks have gone strangely quiet — the same instrument reading,
one valley over, that nobody on-screen remarks on.)

sections.title: 4. The Hush, the Warden, and the Whisperwood — what actually happened
sections.body:
**The Warden of the Kindling** has tended the failing mother-fire alone for three hundred years.
He fed it first with himself — nearly all of his own heart is poured in, every joy and grief he
ever kept, which is why he is hooded, silent, and unhurried, and why his lantern-light burns
**pale blue**: keeping-fire with no one left to warm it burns cold. When he ran out of self, he
began **the recalls**: by the Order's oldest law, every Heartlight is a *loan* from the
Kindling, and a light whose keeper's line has failed is a lamp left burning in an empty house —
moths will scatter it; better the Warden carry the valley's heart home and pour it into the
mother-fire, where it will at least keep *something* burning. He has emptied valley after
valley this way — three centuries of recalls, and the work is nearly complete: Emberbrook was
one of the last names left on his register. He does not consider it theft. He grieves at every
one. He bows.

**Nobody alive knows what he does.** This is a hard rule of the early game: **no one but the
Warden knows what happens when a Heartlight is taken.** There is no literature — the Order that
could have written it dissolved before the first recall ever happened, and its surviving books
(Tally's thirty-nine volumes) are rites, roads, and rules, not casualties. There is no
institutional memory — every valley that could have testified frayed and scattered (see "Why
Ashfield vanished," below). So Emberbrook's Hush plays to *everyone* — villagers, party, and
player alike — as the **first such event in history**: unprecedented, unexplained, without a
name in any book. This illusion is load-bearing for the Chapter Five reveal, and the reveal
order is deliberate: the party first *witnesses* a second recall at Harrowdel (Ch. 4), first
learns there were *earlier* ones at Ashfield (Ch. 5), and first sees the full register at the
Gatehouse (Ch. 8).

**Why Emberbrook, why now:** Grandmother died a year ago, and Lake never swore the vow. For one
year, by the letter of the law the Warden serves, Emberbrook's light stood *unclaimed*. (That
Lake's nightly rounds were a claiming-in-deed is an argument the Warden will lose later — but
he wasn't checking deeds; he was reading the register of vows.) **Why on Emberwake:** the
Kindling Hour is the one hour the Heartlight opens its old line to the mother-fire. The Warden
did not break in; he stood at the far end of an open door and *recalled the loan*. The whole
heart of the village — the warmth in its lamps, the Heartlight, every bond and every banked
year — went down the line into his lantern between one heartbeat and the next. "His lantern
isn't dark — it's FULL." He bowed to the lighter because the lighter is validly claimed: a debt
collector's courtesy toward the one title in the valley he cannot touch. This is also why
Vesper's guilt-free and Lake's arc has a knife in it: Lake will eventually learn that his year
of doubt was the legal opening — and must be talked out of carrying that as fault. The law was
wrong, not the boy.

**Why carrying the Last Spark to the Kindling can fix it:** light is only ever carried — so
Emberbrook's heart is not destroyed; it is *in transit*, held in shape inside the Warden's
lantern, on foot (a full lantern moves at the pace of its carrier; keeping-fire cannot be
rushed). **Once poured into the Kindling, it dissolves into the general fire and can never be
re-separated** — that is the arc's hard deadline, and why the quest is a race, not a chase:
reach the Kindling before the pour. The second clock is slower and crueler: an unanchored
village *frays*. Letters from home arrive flatter, not blanker — all news, no warmth;
commitments quietly dissolving; Mara caring for Pip out of a duty that is wearing thin.
Restoration needs three things: a **sibling ember** (a seed lit from the same mother, to be the
new hearth the warmth can come home to), a **keeper** to hold it meant, and **people who can
re-anchor the village** — who can stand at the rekindled flame and say who everyone is *to each
other*, so the bonds take hold again. Names called at the new flame are bonds renewed, not
records retrieved. This is why Vesper's post-Hush notebook ("a copy of everyone" — who people
are to each other, the seeds of re-anchoring) and Rowan's ledger of facts become, literally,
the key items of the endgame.

**Vesper and Ashfield — the writer-side truth.** (*Ashfield* is a writer-side name. Vesper
does not know it; her parents cannot produce it; no character may say it before Chapter Five.)
Vesper was born in Ashfield, a flame-valley the Warden recalled — Hushed — when she was six;
its light was later poured into the Kindling. Her parents were ordinary Ashfielders. When the
flame left, everything they had banked went with it: the love of the place, the weight of their
own past. Unmoored from everything pre-Hush, they simply kept walking — never went back, never
rehearsed the facts — and unrehearsed facts fogged over the ordinary human way. **Not amnesia:
indifference calcified into fog.** They are warm *present-tense* people: ask about last summer
and you get stories; ask about anything before Vesper was born and you get weather. They told
their daughter the true outline — born in a town, something bad happened, we left — and
honestly cannot give her more. They are alive, settled far south, letters twice a year, no rift
of any kind: just a daughter who couldn't live inside the fog. To Vesper, home is simply "the
town": a well, a fence, a hill, a six-year-old's fragments — and one private wrongness she
tells nobody: she remembers the hill and feels *nothing* about it, and people are supposed to
feel things about home.

**Vesper's dreams:** her name was told to Ashfield's Heartlight at her naming; when that light
was poured into the Kindling, the belonging of six-year-old Vesper — her childhood's whole
feeling of home — went into the mother-fire, and it has been dreaming back at her ever since:
forty-one numbered drawings of the clearing, the road "she had only ever seen with her eyes
closed," the waystone, the gate in the corner of drawing forty-one. The dreams are her one
lead, and she is embarrassed by them — a record-keeper of true things forced to trust the
unverifiable. She half-believes the dream-place is near her hometown; she is righter than she
knows. ("Sending me MAIL" is her sarcasm about exactly this.) The Hush could not touch her
because each flame holds only what it was given — Emberbrook's flame never held a feather's
weight of her. But the Kindling holds a six-year-old's belonging, and it has been sending her
mail for twenty years. Her unexplained certainty in the pact scene — *"It won't."* — is the
piece of her in the fire, answering. **Reveal schedule:** her hometown, its Hush, and her
parents' fog land at Chapter Five; the dream *mechanism* (the name-bond in the mother-fire,
calling) stays a late-game reveal — mechanics confirmed Ch. 8, resolution Ch. 10, when "she
finally dreams her own dreams."

**Why Ashfield vanished from the world:** a hushed town vanishes twice. First in fact: the
unmoored fray and scatter — nobody stays to tend a place nobody feels — and the wood eats its
road. Then in memory: it falls off maps and out of conversation, because towns stay on maps the
way people stay in hearts, and everyone who loved Ashfield had the loving taken. Nobody tells
stories about a place nobody misses. This is why the Hush can be unprecedented *and* not new:
the evidence disperses itself. When the party reaches Ashfield (Ch. 5) it is a ghost town —
intact, grey, silent — and the double reveal lands: **this happened before**, and **this is
Emberbrook's future if they fail.** The name is discovered *at* the reveal (its own waystone,
moss brushed back). Vesper's parents are retroactively exonerated in the same stroke — their
fog was the ordinary shape of an extraordinary wound. This is the scene that crystallizes the
quest's stakes.

sections.title: 5. The Arc — Chapters 2–10
sections.body:
The party travels the Whisperwood along the Order's dead circuit, gaining companions FF-style,
racing a walker who cannot be outrun, only intercepted — while letters from home arrive flatter
and flatter: all news, no warmth. From Chapter Two the road is partly a river: the one road the
wood cannot move.

subs.title: Ch. 2 — Dellhollow
subs.body: NEW first road chapter: a river-gorge lock-town, run entirely on human themes (the lore budget's proof case — youth vs inherited station, a mother who lost a husband to the river her daughter loves, town pride and politics, a nesting animal nobody should fight). *The road down:* Vesper's map is already wrong; the Stranger is glimpsed once across a ravine — he bows, and is gone (this beat MOVES HERE from the Lanternstead chapter). *The rim vista:* the first living town since the Hush — flameless and thriving, which quietly challenges Lake: a town that holds together without any flame. *The jam:* Lock Five is shut — "the Tenant," an enormous ancient river eel, is nesting in the flooded chamber, eggs in the sluice gallery; she came up from the lower river, where the banks have gone strangely quiet (one soft, unremarked echo). **Maren**, 17, the lock-keeper's daughter, best unlicensed pilot on the river, a charcoal tally of run rapids up her forearm; her dream is to pilot the Long Reach — the wild stretch north through the deep wood, unrun in living memory — the stretch that took her father, whose unfinished chart of it, drawn from memory the night before he went, is locked away by her mother. **Odessa**, guildmother, iron, runs the locks. *The flume plan:* the old dry log-chute through the cliff; two winch-crews working in time (co-op); Odessa catches them mid-attempt — and takes a winch herself. *The flume run:* the chapter's spectacle, ending in the held-breath glide past the Tenant's watching eye — no fight. *The chart handoff:* Odessa gives the dead pilot's wrong chart to VESPER — "she'll want to fix it; don't let her do that alone." Maren joins; the party gains its first BOAT; end on the river north — water holds its course, and the river is the only fixed road through the unmappable wood (this is also Vesper's craft-revelation scene). Vesper threads: the dock scene with Lake (her real biography, conversationally — the nameless town, the fog, the search); Maren's innocent "so where are YOU from?" — Vesper has no answer, in front of a girl who is too rooted (their friendship engine: the girl who can't leave home and the woman who never had one); the chart-handoff rhyme. Lake: seasick; the lighter shielded on water; the quiet kinship scene with Odessa — two keepers of daily duties. Mochi despises the boat, then claims the prow. Night-watchman line: a pale blue light on the far bank, two nights ago.

subs.title: Ch. 3 — The Lanternstead
subs.body: Formerly Chapter Two; full script in `docs/chapter2-script.md`, all content preserved. The pair learn the road lamp to lamp — Lake relights the Order's dead road-lamps, moths pooling in the dark stretches. At dusk they reach the Order's waystation and **Tally** — round, bespectacled, endlessly cheerful last friar, keeper of every rite and a great-lantern he has never seen burn ("You're real. The office is real. I have the whole liturgy and nobody ever came."). The well that takes two; the night's first moth swarm comes for the lighter; the pair relight the great-lantern together, wick and winch; Tally weeps laughing at his first living light, and the lighter travels hooded after dark. Rowan's first letter arrives by grey post-crow — perfectly informative, strangely flat. Tally joins; the road points to Harrowdel. Noted adjustments: the Stranger-glimpse beat STAYS here as the SECOND sighting (Ch. 2's ravine glimpse is the first; Mochi's line becomes "Twice in his life now — both times at that silhouette"; the escalation is glimpse → rumor → this clearer look at the pale-blue lantern), and the party now arrives WITH Maren and the boat. Status: implemented in-engine as Chapter Three (`public/js/chapter3.js`); content otherwise not reworked in the near term.

subs.title: Ch. 4 — Harrowdel
subs.body: Formerly Chapter Three, content unchanged: a living valley — one of the last — whose elderly keeper is failing; slow decline shown in miniature, the stakes made ordinary. The Warden arrives and recalls Harrowdel before their eyes; first face-to-face; first bow. **Sable**, Harrowdel's heretic moth-catcher — who traps strays and returns stray warmth to people jar by jar — joins in cold fury. The old Ferry chapter is folded into the road fabric here or en route: **Marrow** the ferryman — payment only in memories, rich and numb — is now an en-route encounter, not a chapter; the party pays, Marrow *feels* the memory (first thing in years) and poles after them. Keep his hook ("You've paid me before. Long ago. Small girl, going the other way.") and his debt-of-feeling payoff at Ch. 8. Maren pilots the legs between: the Long Reach is the party's route north.

subs.title: Ch. 5 — Ashfield
subs.body: The ghost town: intact, grey, silent — found off Maren's father's wrong chart, where the wood ate the road. The midpoint chapter and the arc's stakes-scene: the name discovered at its own waystone; the double reveal (this happened before / this is Emberbrook's future if they fail); Vesper's birthplace — her parents' fog explained and exonerated in the same stroke, and the world's first knowledge of what happens when a Heartlight is taken. Mochi finds the house. The lighter flares at the cold pedestal. Vesper's chapter; Lake holds the lantern-watch and finally says grandmother's sayings out loud as *teaching*, not grief. She half-connects the dreams to home; the full mechanism waits (Ch. 8).

subs.title: Ch. 6 — The Parley
subs.body: The Warden speaks. The Kindling is dying; the last few living Heartlights die with it; he offers Lake the succession — Warden after him — and asks for the lighter as the last free seed. The creed schism made explicit: same creed, opposite obligations. Refusal costs them: home letters arrive drained — news without one feeling in it. Lake learns about the unclaimed year and takes the knife of it.

subs.title: Ch. 7 — The Mothway
subs.body: Lake's chapter, opening on the Parley's knife still in him. The lighter is lost — or guttering; the chapter keeps it ambiguous whether Lake, wounded by the unclaimed year, half-let it happen ("maybe it was never mine"). For the first time the grey of the road reaches *him*: unmoored-Lake, facts intact, caring gone. **Vesper re-anchors him** — the "copy of everyone" notebook gets its first use on her own partner, the record-keeper of true things telling a keeper who he is, page by page, until it lands *felt* again — and helps him recover the lighter and the mission. Resolution: he relights it **from within**, finally understanding "mean it" — the flame was never the brass, the meaning was always *spending something true* — and that same understanding *is* the discovery of the counter-move: amid the storm-migration of moths (revealed: not vermin — stray unanchored *feeling*), Sable's craft plus Lake's newly meant fire learns that a meant flame calls moths home the way lamps ward them off. Stray warmth of Emberbrook is recovered and sent back. One crisis, three payoffs. *(Design note: deliberate mirror — Ch. 5 Ashfield is Vesper's identity crisis with Lake anchoring her; Ch. 7 is Lake's with Vesper anchoring him. Each saves the other exactly once — couch co-op thematics.)*

subs.title: Ch. 8 — The Gatehouse
subs.body: The Warden's home: the archive of poured valleys, the ledger of recalls — three centuries of them, Ashfield's line among them — and the almost-empty man himself glimpsed off-duty. The mechanism of restoration is confirmed — a sibling ember, a keeper to hold it, and people to re-anchor the village name by name — turning Vesper's notebook and Rowan's ledger into the endgame keys. The dream mechanism is confirmed here too: Vesper's name-bond, poured with Ashfield's light into the mother-fire, has been calling her for twenty years. Marrow repays his debt of feeling.

subs.title: Ch. 9 — The Pouring
subs.body: The clearing of drawing forty-one. The Kindling is nearly ash. The Warden begins the pour; the party arrives mid-stream; and the true dilemma lands — stopping him saves Emberbrook and starves the mother-fire, dooming the last living valleys. Nobody is wrong. Cliffhanger on the choice.

subs.title: Ch. 10 — The Long Rekindling
subs.body: The answer was on the mantel all along: *it was never hers — or his — to put out, because it was always meant to go home.* Lake can return the lighter to the stone bowl because after Chapter Seven he is a keeper who no longer needs the vessel — the flame was never the brass. The seed — three centuries of nightly meant-fire, freely kept — rekindles the mother-flame. Emberbrook is called home out of the lantern name by name — every name a bond renewed at the new flame, not a record retrieved — Vesper and Rowan's ink the seeds of re-anchoring (Pip first). The Warden pours the last of himself to bridge the rekindling, and the party tells the new fire his story — so the weight of him stays warm in the light. The fix is structural, not magical: the circuit is walked again — and part-sailed: Maren runs the Long Reach as its pilot, her father's chart finished and corrected in Vesper's hand. Vesper — Waykeeper — charts the circuit; her piece comes home; she finally dreams her own dreams. Coda: next Emberwake, two keepers at the Kindling Hour, Mochi on the pedestal.

subs.title: Vesper
subs.body:
**Vesper** — traveling mapmaker, late twenties, eleven days' walk from anywhere on her charts.
Professional creed: *keep records of true things.* Quick-tongued, precise, allergic to
mysticism. Her motivation, stated simply: **she is looking for the town where she was born, and
she has no name for it and no direction to it** — which is why eleven years of searching have
failed, and why the mission is hard. Her parents left the town when she was six; to her it is
just "the town," her nameless home (writer-side: Ashfield — she does not know the name, and
neither, truly, do they). She carries a six-year-old's fragments — a well, a fence, a hill —
and one private wrongness she tells no one: she remembers the hill and feels *nothing* about
it, and people are supposed to feel things about home. Her parents are alive, settled far
south, warm, and useless as witnesses — present-tense people: ask about last summer, you get
stories; ask about before she was born, you get weather. They told her the true outline — born
in a town, something bad happened, we left — and honestly cannot give her more. No rift:
letters twice a year, love on both sides; just a daughter who couldn't live inside the fog.
Mapmaking is the profession she *chose* because of all this: route-walking pays — maps are
perishable, the wood shifts roads, mapmakers walk and certify routes because copying old maps
gets people killed, and she walks every mile she draws — and every route walked is a search
grid. The blank at the center of every map is personal. The dreams are her one lead, and she is
embarrassed by them: since age six she has drawn the same clearing in her sleep, forty-one
numbered times, and refuses to be strange about it. She half-believes the dream-place is near
her hometown; drawing forty-one had a real gate in the corner, which is what brought her to
Emberbrook ("Something has been sending me MAIL. I intend to answer it in person" — sarcasm as
armor for trusting the unverifiable). What she doesn't know: the town is Ashfield and it was
Hushed (Ch. 5), and the dreams are her own name-bond calling from the mother-fire, wanting its
Waykeeper home (confirmed Ch. 8; resolved Ch. 10 — she finally dreams her own dreams). Her arc
runs from charting places to charting *people* — the post-Hush notebook, "a copy of everyone,"
who they are and who they are to each other, begun in stubborn pity, is used first on the
villagers, then (Ch. 7, its proving) on her own unmoored partner, and finally as the literal
instrument of the ending. Signature habits: numbers her drawings, counts her honeybuns
dishonestly, writes things down mid-catastrophe. *(Deleted from canon: the lamp-counting
mother; the parents-as-vigilant-protectors version; any newcomers-who-banked-little framing.)*

subs.title: Lake
subs.body:
**Lake** — the last lamplighter of Emberbrook, mid-twenties, quiet, apologizes to lamps. Raised
by his grandmother in the Order's household discipline: the rounds, the sayings, the cat, and
the one hard rule he only half-understood — *we don't tell the flame; we keep our own.* Which
means his grief is different from everyone else's: they give their griefs to the flame and are
given warmth back; his grandmother's loss lives only in him, whole and unshared, in a handful of
sayings he can't stop reciting. A year ago she set the lighter down; he picked it up and did the
rounds every dusk since — but never swore the vow, because he wasn't sure he believed, and it
felt like lying to a fire. What being lamplighter means to him at curtain-rise: a costume he's
wearing for a dead woman. The road sharpens the doubt before it heals it: Dellhollow — the
first living town the party meets — is flameless and thriving, and a town that holds together
without any flame quietly asks him what a keeper is *for*; the question travels with him to
Ch. 7, where the answer turns out to be the counter-move itself. The doubt comes due twice: his
unsworn year turns out to be the Warden's legal opening (the Ch. 6 knife), and in Chapter Seven
it nearly takes the lighter itself — his crisis, mirrored on Vesper's at Ashfield, and it is
Vesper who anchors him back (that is the story's job, and mostly hers: make him put the knife
down). What the office means by Chapter Ten: the thing he was quietly doing all along —
carrying, no vessel required. He knows every window in the village and every name behind it,
because remembering people the hard way is the family trade.

subs.title: Rowan
subs.body:
**Rowan** — village elder and ledger-keeper; runs Emberbrook by cheerfully denying he runs it.
Forty years of ledger live in his hands, which is why "I keep the ledger" is the one thing that
still feels true after the Hush — while his own name sits in his mouth like a borrowed word.
Practical in catastrophe, funny on purpose, and the closest thing to an Order scholar the valley
has left — he knows the *words* of the old rites without their reasons.

subs.title: Poppy
subs.body:
**Poppy** — baker; hospitality-as-law ("guests eat first — that is LAW on Emberwake"); burns her
thumb on the first tray every morning and swears she won't tomorrow. Post-Hush she rebuilds
herself from a row of words: *honeybuns, Poppy, thumb.*

subs.title: Mara
subs.body:
**Mara** — Pip's mother; steady, unsentimental warmth; waited out a snowstorm at the pass for
him to be born. Post-Hush she believes every fact about her son and feels none of them — the
Hush's cruelest exhibit, and the engine of the letters-from-home thread.

subs.title: Pip
subs.body:
**Pip** — seven; orbits people he likes; owns one good stick. The first villager to put the
counter-move into practice: "I'm teaching her me again." Believes in ink because Vesper told him
to; the rekindled flame gives him his mother back — her love of him renewed by name — in
Chapter Ten, first.

subs.title: Finn
subs.body:
**Finn** — fisherman; prefers fish to festivals; hands know the knots. The valley's accidental
instrument-reader: his circling fish are the only warning anyone got.

subs.title: Mochi
subs.body:
**Mochi** — grandmother's cat, technically; left the cottage the year she died and hired himself
to Vesper at the waystone (a cat recognizing a Waykeeper is old Order staffing procedure).
Moth-warden: sees them, hunts them, keeps them off sleepers. Keeps his entire self in the body,
where moths can't reach. Acknowledges Lake as staff. Despises the boat on principle for exactly
one day, then claims the prow permanently.

subs.title: Maren
subs.body:
**Maren** — seventeen, lock-keeper's daughter of Dellhollow; best unlicensed pilot on the
river, and cheerfully unrepentant about the "unlicensed." Quick, loud, fearless on water and
awkward off it; allergic to being told what she'll want when she's older. Up her left forearm
runs a charcoal tally, one mark per rapid she has run — her records, worn on the skin: set her
against Vesper as mirrored navigators, the woman who inks every mile into a notebook and the
girl who wears every rapid on her arm; they recognize each other before they like each other.
Her dream is the Long Reach — the wild stretch north through the deep wood, unrun in living
memory — the stretch that took her father. He drew an unfinished chart of it from memory the
night before he went; her mother keeps it locked away. The mother is Odessa, and the friction
between them has no villain: a mother who lost a husband to the river her daughter loves, and a
daughter whose dream is the exact shape of the family wound. Traveling with the party is her
dream's first step said out loud — somebody must pilot them north, the Reach is the route, and
she is the only living soul who wants it. What she gives the party: the boat, the river, and an
outsider's plain questions ("so where are YOU from?") that keep the themes honest — her
rootedness against Vesper's namelessness is their friendship engine: the girl who can't leave
home and the woman who never had one. What the party gives her: a way to leave home that isn't
a betrayal of it.

subs.title: Odessa
subs.body:
**Odessa** — guildmother of the Dellhollow locks; iron, fair, unhurried; runs the river the way
Rowan runs the ledger. Widow of the river's best pilot; keeps his unfinished chart of the Long
Reach locked away, and her grief undeclared. Catches the party mid-flume-scheme — and takes a
winch herself. Her parting handoff of the dead pilot's wrong chart to Vesper — "she'll want to
fix it; don't let her do that alone" — is her whole heart, said once, sideways. Quiet kinship
scene with Lake: two keepers of daily duties.

subs.title: The Tenant
subs.body:
**The Tenant** — an enormous, ancient river eel nesting in Lock Five's flooded chamber, eggs in
the sluice gallery. Not a monster and never fought: a wild animal in the wrong place, handled
with a river-town's professional respect. She came up from the lower river, where the banks
have gone strangely quiet — a soft echo nobody on-screen remarks on. The held-breath glide past
her watching eye is the flume run's crown.

subs.title: The Warden (the Stranger)
subs.body:
**The Warden (the Stranger)** — last officer of the Order, keeper of the Kindling, three hundred
years at his post, nearly all of himself poured into the fire he serves. Pale-blue lantern:
keeping-fire with no one left to warm it. Courteous the way a debt collector is courteous; bows
to valid claims; grieves every recall and performs the next one. Not evil — *loyal*, to the
letter of a law written for a world that still walked its circuits. He is what a lamplighter
becomes who keeps the flame and lets go of everything the flame was for. He is also, until
Chapter Ten, the only person alive who knows the whole truth — including the world's only
knowledge of what happens to a village when its Heartlight is taken.

sections.title: 7. What the Player Should Understand When
sections.body:
**Plain knowledge — villagers say this casually, like weather.** Lamps keep the streets
warm-hearted; a dark lamp means a dull, grey-feeling morning. At Emberwake you bring a memory
worth keeping and tell it to the flame; you keep the memory, the flame keeps the heart of it
forever and shines it back on everybody, as long as you live in the light. The lamplighter's
family tends the flame, does the rounds at dusk, and — everyone knows this, nobody thinks it's
odd — never makes tellings themselves. Moths are bad news; cats are good news; nothing starts
until the lamps are lit. Kids' folklore is accurate: Renn is *right* that you can see the
tellings go in. And this, everywhere on the road: **most of the world has no flame at all** —
flameless towns are the ordinary case, living on plain human warmth, and fire-villages are
rare, remote, and half-legendary: places most people have heard of and never seen. **Dialogue
rule: none of this is mysterious to anyone on-screen. Write it warm, proud, and
matter-of-fact.**

**Tradition — known but not understood.** Why the keeper stands outside the kept; why the
lighter never goes out; "it wasn't hers to put out"; "light is only ever carried"; the Old
Gate's rules, the twin sigils, and "that road was meant to be walked by two"; "our flame was
first drawn from a shrine deep in the Whisperwood." Villagers repeat these like proverbs —
grandmother's sayings, Rowan's rites — with total confidence and no idea of the machinery.
**Dialogue rule: characters state these plainly and are cheerfully unable to explain them.**

**Mystery — reserved, with reveal chapters.** Moths are stray feeling (7, seeded from Ch. 1).
What happens when a Heartlight is taken — **nobody alive knows**; Emberbrook's Hush plays as
the first such event in history until the road proves otherwise (witnessed again at 4, revealed
as old at 5). Vesper's hometown, its name, and why her parents can't remember (5). The parents'
fog is Hush aftermath — **WRITING RULE:** in early and mid chapters, the parents' vagueness
must read as ordinary immigrant-parent haziness, and is NEVER described in hush-anatomy terms
("kept facts / lost feelings" phrasing is forbidden for them before Ch. 5), so players cannot
connect the dots early. The recall law and the unclaimed year (6). The Warden's identity,
history, and the dying Kindling (6–8). Vesper's dreams as her own name-bond calling from the
mother-fire (seeded by Marrow en route in 4; mechanics confirmed 8; resolved 10). Re-anchoring
— names as bonds renewed at a sibling flame — as the mechanism of restoration (8). The lighter
as the Kindling's own seed and the meaning of returning it (10). **Dialogue rule: nobody hints
at these early — the early chapters play fair by being genuinely warm and plain, so the Hush
lands on a real village, not a spooky one.**

sections.title: Canon quick-reference (for writers)
sections.body:
1. Light does not die; it is only ever carried. Every plot beat obeys this — including the Hush,
   which is a *transfer*, and the ending, which is a *return*.
2. A flame keeps only what it is told — and what it keeps is the *feeling*, never the fact.
   Memory stays with its owner, always and everywhere; the flame pools the given-over heart of
   things and rains it back through the lamps. Ink keeps facts; bodies keep skills; only flame
   keeps meaning — and what the flame keeps, the flame can lose, or have carried away.
3. The Hush is unmooring, never amnesia. Post-Hush villagers know every fact and feel none of
   them. Write dissociation ("it doesn't feel like mine"), never failed recall ("I can't
   remember the fact").
4. Keepers stand outside the kept: lamplighters never tell the flame, so they alone carry their
   own hearts whole — and grieve the old way.
5. Claiming is keeping: the lighter stays lit, and untouchable, because Lake carried it — vow or
   no vow. The law reads registers; the fire reads deeds. The fire is right.
6. The Warden is owed sympathy at every appearance. He bows. He is never sneered at by the
   script, only argued with.
7. Flames are rare. The Order seeded widely; the circuit died; the keepers' lines failed; the
   Warden has spent three centuries recalling the orphaned lights and is nearly done. A handful
   remain. Most of the world is flameless and perfectly fine.
8. The Hush is unprecedented in living knowledge. Nobody alive but the Warden knows what
   happens when a Heartlight is taken — no book, no witness, no proverb. Emberbrook's Hush
   plays as the first in history; the truth waits for Ashfield (Ch. 5).
9. Vesper's parents' fog is Hush-fog — revealed at Ch. 5 and never telegraphed early. Before
   then, their vagueness is written as ordinary immigrant-parent haziness, never in
   hush-anatomy terms.
10. The lore budget: one deep fictional system — the flame institution and its appendages
    (moths, the Warden, the shifting wood). Everything else runs on familiar, relatable human
    themes. No new invented systems without explicit director sign-off.

chapters.title: Chapter One — Emberwake
chapters.sub: a mapmaker, a lamplighter, and the night the village forgot itself

beats.title: Opening — Vesper
beats.summary: On the last night of autumn, a mapmaker follows a road she has only ever dreamed.

lines: — VESPER — — a mapmaker with someone else’s dreams
On the last night of autumn, a mapmaker walked into a valley that was not on her maps — following a road she had only ever seen with her eyes closed.

beats.title: The waystone & Mochi
beats.summary: The waystone from drawing forty-one is real, and a cat decides something about Vesper.

lines: (A waystone. A worn face carved into the stone — patient, half-swallowed by moss, watching the road.)
(It’s the one from drawing forty-one. Line for line, crack for crack.)
(Which is impossible. I drew it eleven days ago, four hundred miles from here — asleep.)
GAH— …a cat. Hello. You’re not in my records.
(The cat has decided something about me. I am choosing to find that flattering.)
(Also noted: lamplight, up the road. Eleven days of cold camps, and this valley feels like walking into somebody’s kitchen.)
(There will be an explanation. There is always an explanation.)

blocks.context: looking at the waystone (flavor, by role)
lines: The waystone from drawing forty-one. She has stopped checking whether it matches. It matches.
The old waystone, watching the road since before the village had a name. Somebody has recently brushed the moss from its eyes.

beats.title: The square — Emberwake festival
beats.summary: Vesper meets the villagers on festival night and learns what the Kindling Hour is.

blocks.context: talking to Poppy as Vesper, pre-Hush (the telling explained)
lines: A new face! Here, take a bun, love. Nobody goes hungry in Emberbrook on Emberwake — that’s not kindness, that’s LAW.
I— fine. One. For the record: I’m here to chart the Whisperwood, not to eat pastry.
Chart the— HA! Did you hear her? Eat two.
And where’s home for you, love? Everybody’s road starts somewhere.
Nowhere, yet. It’s under survey. Ask me again when the survey’s done.
My turn for a question. Everyone keeps saying “the Kindling Hour” like I was born knowing what it is.
Simplest thing in the world, love. Once a year you bring your best memory and TELL it to the flame.
You keep the memory. The flame keeps the warmth of it — and shines it back on every soul in the lamplight, forever.
You give your memories. To a fire. On purpose.
Give? TELL, love. You lose nothing — you keep every minute. The flame only takes what it meant to you.
And it pays that back to all of us, all year. Why do you think nobody here stays angry past sundown?
(That warm feeling, ever since the waystone. It isn’t the weather. It’s coming out of the LAMPS.)
(I ate two. In my defense: eleven days of walking, and they were extraordinary.)

blocks.context: talking to Mara & Pip as Vesper, pre-Hush
lines: Pip, love, stop orbiting the nice stranger.
Are you a REAL mapmaker? Have you been everywhere?
Have you been to the MOON?
He’ll remember tonight his whole life. Some grown-up Emberwake, he’ll tell it to the flame.
He’ll keep every minute of it — and the whole village will sleep warmer for his good night. Nights like this are what winters are for.

blocks.context: talking to Rowan as Vesper before greeting two villagers
lines: A guest! Guests eat first, my dear — that is LAW on Emberwake, ask the baker. Come back fed and greeted.

blocks.context: talking to Rowan as Vesper, pre-Hush (leads into Vesper’s outro)
lines: Now then. A guest, on Emberwake of all nights! Nobody finds Emberbrook by accident, my dear.
Vesper. Mapmaker. I need to get through your gate, and I’m told you’re in charge.
In charge! Ha! I keep the ledger; the village keeps itself.
And nobody opens the Old Gate, child. It hasn’t a key. It has RULES.
Rules can be charted. Charting things is my whole profession.
…Ha! I like you. Stay for the Kindling Hour, mapmaker — one hour, the whole village, the year’s best told to the flame.
Nobody should walk into the Whisperwood on an empty heart.
Three hundred years that flame has burned. In all of them, nobody in this valley has carried a grief alone.
That is not boasting, my dear. It is bookkeeping. I checked.
As for the Gate — lamplighter business. His order built it, his order shut it. Ask HIM.
The quiet one with the flame that never goes out. You’ll find him apologizing to lamps somewhere across the village.

blocks.context: talking to Mochi, pre-Hush — as Vesper
lines: You’re still here. I don’t feed you. I have never fed you.

blocks.context: the notice board (flavor, before/after the Hush)
lines: The notice board. Everyone can still read every word. The words have just stopped mattering to anyone but you two.
One notice reads: "LOST — brown dog, answers to Biscuit."
The notice board. "EMBERWAKE TONIGHT — bring a memory worth keeping. And a chair. We are short of chairs."
The Heartlight of Emberbrook. Every street lamp in the village is lit from this one flame.
Three hundred years of the village’s heart, warm inside it — given over and shone back. It hums, very faintly, like a kettle two rooms away.
What is left of the Heartlight. It does not hum. It does not do anything.
Holding a hand near it feels like reading a love letter meant for somebody else.

beats.title: Interlude — the third honeybun
beats.summary: Vesper waits for the lamps; the story turns to the other side of the village.

blocks.context: cutscene — Vesper’s outro / Lake’s title card (playVesperOutro)
lines: The mapmaker bought a third honeybun she had no intention of admitting to, and waited for the lamps.
— LAKE — — the last lamplighter of Emberbrook

beats.title: Lake — the cottage
beats.summary: The last lamplighter takes down his grandmother’s flame, a year after she set it down.

lines: The same evening, on the other side of the village: the last lamplighter of Emberbrook rose from his grandmother’s table, and took down his grandmother’s flame.
(A year tonight since she set the lighter down and didn’t pick it up again.)
(Forty years she carried it. It has never once gone out.)
(She used to say it wasn’t hers to put out. I used to think that was a saying.)
(The job, the way she gave it to me: carry the flame from the village’s heart to a lamppost near every home.)
(So nobody sleeps outside the warmth. She said it the way you’d say “fetch the water.”)
(Three lamps left before the Kindling Hour. Best go and mean it.)
The hearth. Grandmother’s portrait watches from above the mantel — dusted daily, and the eyes still miss nothing.
Beneath it, an empty brass hook, worn bright. The lighter’s place, between rounds.

beats.title: The rounds
beats.summary: Three dark lamps before the Kindling Hour — and the pond has started acting strangely.

lines: (One. The wick takes the flame like it remembers it — Grandmother swore they do.)
(While this lamp burns, every house on the lane sits inside the village’s heart. Dark lamp, dull street.)
(Two. “Light them like you mean it,” she said, “or they gutter by midnight.”)
(She never once explained what the meaning involved. I improvise.)

blocks.context: lighting the last lamp — the ring closed
lines: (Three. The ring is closed before full dark, same as every night of my life.)
(A lit lamp near every door. Wherever you sleep in Emberbrook tonight, you sleep inside the warmth.)

blocks.context: talking to Finn in the lane, pre-Hush (Vesper/Lake variants)
lines: Festival’s up in the square. Fish are down here. I know which conversation I prefer.
Somethin’s got the pond spooked tonight, though. They’re swimmin’ in a circle. One big circle. All of ’em. Slow.
Fish don’t… do that. Do they?
They ever done that before, Finn?
Didn’t think so either, this morning.

blocks.context: talking to Poppy as Lake, pre-Hush
lines: There he is! The one soul allowed to be late tonight — nothing starts till his lamps are lit. Bun?
Half a bun. I’ll hold the other half hostage until the Kindling Hour.
No use asking if YOU’LL make a telling this year. Keeper keeps his own — I know, I know. Strangest rule your family ever kept. Saddest one, too.
(Everyone else gives their year to the flame, and the flame carries it for them. Keepers carry their own.)
(“Someone has to stand outside the kept,” she said. I never asked why.)

blocks.context: talking to Rowan as Lake, pre-Hush
lines: Lake! The Kindling Hour will not wait for poetry. Lamps, boy, lamps!

blocks.context: talking to Mara & Pip as Lake, pre-Hush
lines: He’s been up since dawn, Lake. He’ll sleep where he falls, and I’ll carry him home like every year.
Renn says you can SEE the memories go in. I’m staying awake to check.
Remember this night, both of you. That’s what it’s for.

blocks.context: talking to Mochi, pre-Hush — as Lake
lines: (Mochi is escorting the stranger with the interesting satchel. He acknowledges you, lamplighter, as staff.)

blocks.context: blocked ways (denied-exit lines, shown when a road can’t be taken)
lines: The south road can wait. Something is happening HERE.
Lane’s dark and empty. Everyone’s in the square tonight.
The Old Gate. Nobody goes that way… it’s been shut my whole life.

beats.title: The meet
beats.summary: The mapmaker finds the lamplighter — just as the Kindling Hour is called.

lines: Excuse me. You look official — you’re holding fire. Are you the lamplighter, or do I keep collecting cats until one talks?
Oh. Um. Yes? The first one. Lake. The cats don’t talk, to my knowledge.
Vesper. Mapmaker. Your elder says the Old Gate is your family’s business, and I need it open.
The— nobody crosses the Gate. It hasn’t opened in my lifetime. Why would anyone want—
Because this valley isn’t on any chart I own. And I walked here anyway — on a road I only know from dreams.
Something on the other side of that gate has been sending me MAIL, Lake. I intend to answer it in person.
The HOUR! Gather, gather! Neighbors, to the square! Lake — the flame!

beats.title: The Kindling Hour & the Hush
beats.summary: The village brings its year to the flame — and between two heartbeats, the light leaves.

blocks.context: cutscene — the festival and the catastrophe (playKindlingHour)
lines: Three hundred years, and the warmth of every one of them — right here. Every wedding. Every quarrel mended. Every good loaf and bad winter.
You know the trade, and I never tire of saying it: bring the year’s best and tell it to the flame.
You keep the memory — every minute, yours till you die. The flame keeps the WARMTH, and shines it on all of us, for good.
What we tell the flame, the flame keeps.
So bring your year, neighbors — the good and the bad of it. Let the flame hold it safe.
The flood! The spring flood — the whole town in my bakery, bailing water with soup pots, and LAUGHING, gods help us—
It did not happen slowly. Between one heartbeat and the next, the light of Emberbrook — three hundred years of it — stood up and left.
…My stall. My bread. Why doesn’t any of it feel like MINE?
…I’m sorry — whose child— no. I know whose. Mine.
I can say it, and it weighs NOTHING— someone’s crying. Why is someone crying?
Mama. It’s me. It’s Pip. You KNOW me!
Everyone stay where you are. Names! Say your names, out loud — say them NOW.
…I know my name. It just doesn’t feel like mine to say.
I keep the ledger. I know that I keep the ledger.
Every window in this village. Every name behind it. Why do I still— why do WE still—
You two. The stranger and the lamplighter. Everyone in this square is a stranger wearing a neighbor’s face — except you.
Why do YOU still hold your names?
I got here an HOUR ago.
And keepers donu2019t make tellings. We keep our own u2014 Grandmotheru2019s one hard rule.
Whatever took the villageu2019s heartu2026 nothing of mine was ever in it.
…My lighter’s still warm. Every other flame in Emberbrook just died. Not this one.
Then we are not finished. Not yet.
See to them — all of them. They deserve their names back, even borrowed.
Then come find me. I will be with my ledger — every word of it true, and not one of them mine.
The Hush has come to Emberbrook — the village loses its heart

beats.title: Aftermath — seeing to the village
beats.summary: Two strangers give the villagers their names back, borrowed, one by one.

blocks.context: talking to Rowan before seeing to everyone, post-Hush
lines: See to them first. All of them. They deserve to hear their names from someone who still owns them.
I will be here, reading my ledger. The ink is all still there — it is the caring that’s going. Be quick as kindness allows.
…My stall. My bread. My hands — I know every burn scar on them. Why does none of it feel like MINE?
You’re Poppy. You bake. Every morning you burn your thumb on the first tray and swear you won’t tomorrow.
The round warm things. I know the word — it just sounds like nobody’s when I say it. Say it to me, love.
HONEYBUNS. Say more words. Both of you.
Anything in this stall, it’s yours — I’m reliably informed it’s mine to give.
(Writing it all down. Who they are. Who they are to each other.)
(If this ever happens again — there will be a copy of everyone.)
Honeybuns. Poppy. Thumb. I’m keeping the words in a row where I can see them.
My name… I can say the word, friend. It just isn’t MINE anymore. Hands still know the knots, though. Funny what stays.
Tell you one thing for your book, mapmaker: the fish stopped circlin’. The very moment it happened. Like they’d been countin’ down to it.
Didn’t think so either. This morning I was wrong about a lot of things.
Finn. Still short. Still like it.
Mara. This is Pip. Your son. Seven years old. You waited out a snowstorm at the pass for him to be born.
I believe you. That is the worst of it — I believe every word, and it lands like a fact about a stranger.
…You held my hand. TONIGHT. You said I’d remember tonight forever.
Pip. Look at me. I saw her holding your hand — an hour ago, by the stall.
I’m a mapmaker. I keep records of true things. And I am telling you: it is TRUE. It happened. I have it.
Whoever you two are — whatever you still carry — do not waste it. Please.

blocks.context: talking to Mara & Pip again, post-Hush (repeat)
lines: I’m teaching her me again. We started with my name and the good stick I found in spring.
It is a very good stick. I have decided to believe in the stick.
The cat. The cat is FINE?!
(Mochi is purring. Mochi has, if anything, improved.)
Cats don’t keep their hearts where moths can reach. My grandmother used to say that.
Your grandmother said that. Casually. As common knowledge.
She said a lot of things. She was a lamplighter too.
Mrrrrp. (He is watching the north road. He does not usually watch anything.)

beats.title: The pact
beats.summary: Rowan reads the fading ledger, names the Kindling, and binds two keepers to one flame.

blocks.context: Rowan, if only one keeper comes to him
lines: Both of you. This concerns the mapmaker AND the lamplighter — I’ll not say it twice.

blocks.context: cutscene — the pact by the Heartlight (playPact)
lines: Look at this. The year four-twenty-nine. The flood. A wedding.
I wrote every line of this page — and tonight I read it like the minutes of somebody else’s village.
Ink keeps facts. It cannot keep what they WEIGHED.
If our flame is truly gone, this village will stand here fed, housed, correct — and never be Emberbrook again.
…I don’t know why I said that with such confidence. Ignore me.
No. Hold on to that, girl; we will need it.
Now — our flame was first drawn from a shrine deep in the Whisperwood. The Kindling.
Every Heartlight in every valley is a child of that fire.
And the old Order kept one creed above all: light does not die — it is only ever carried.
Whatever THAT was tonight, it did not put our flame out, children. It carried it off. Whole.
Three hundred years of us, burning somewhere it should not be.
Nobody knows the way. The Gate’s been shut three hundred years. The road’s gone.
…I need to show you both something, and I need you to not be strange about it.
Since I was six years old, I have drawn one clearing. Over and over. In dreams. Forty-one drawings of the same clearing.
I came here because the forty-first had YOUR gate in the corner.
Girl… that is the Kindling. That is the heart of the Whisperwood.
No. It has been CALLING you. Forty-one times it called.
And tonight of all nights, a mapmaker with the road in her head stands in our square — beside the last living flame in the valley.
The map does not know fire. The flame does not know the way. So it must be both of you, together.
Then you have an hour’s head start on resenting each other. Marvelous. The sigils will want more than acquaintance, mind.
Understand what you carry, boy. That little flame is the last live fire in the valley.
While it burns, Emberbrook is not dead — only dark. Our whole heart went somewhere tonight. Someone must go after it.
The old rite, then. Two keepers, one flame. Say it and mean it, or the Gate will know the difference:
“What they forgot, we keep. What we keep, we return.”
It was not a question, boy. Some decisions are made over our heads.
That cat has been watching the north road all evening. I suggest you take the hint.
✦ Quest — The Long Rekindling ✦ — Carry the Last Spark to the Kindling, deep in the Whisperwood
Twin sigils, before the Gate. The Lamplighters cut them, in the old days — that road was always meant to be walked by two.

blocks.context: talking to Rowan after the pact (repeat)
lines: Twin sigils, before the Gate. Two keepers, one flame. Walk close, and walk kindly.

beats.title: The gate & the sigils
beats.summary: Two keepers stand the twin sigils together, and the Old Gate opens.

blocks.context: objective & prompt text at the Old Gate
lines: Stand on the twin sigils before the Old Gate — together
Step through the Old Gate — together

beats.title: The ending — beyond the gate
beats.summary: The road beyond runs grey with moths, and the first lamp of the long road waits.

blocks.context: cutscene — the road beyond the gate (playEnding)
lines: Beyond the Old Gate, the road ran grey — soft and wrong, like snow that refused to melt.
That’s where the light went. Some of it never made it to the sky.
The moths drifted without hurry and without direction, the way lost things drift.
New page. “North of Emberbrook: one road, unmapped, grey. Full of lost light.”
(I have wanted an unmapped road my whole life. I imagined it differently.)
My grandmother had a saying for the rounds. The first lamp is the whole job — the rest is only more of it.
Then it goes in the book. Come on, partner — first lamp.
The Order of Lamplighters kept one creed: light does not die — it is only ever carried. And on the grey road north, theirs was the last light walking.

beats.title: End card
beats.summary: End of Chapter One.

lines: A mapmaker who dreams of roads. A lamplighter with the last warm flame.
A village that will forget it ever existed — unless they remember it back.

chapters.title: Chapter Two — Dellhollow
chapters.sub: a river-gorge lock-town, a nesting tenant, and the first boat

beats.title: The descent
beats.summary: The road steps off the edge of the world — cut switchbacks, and the other thing.

lines: They walked until the last light of Emberbrook was gone behind them, and slept badly under a bramble. The first morning of winter came up grey and stayed grey.
Half a morning north of the Gate, the old stone road did something no road on Vesper’s charts had ever done. It stepped off the edge of the world.
(Mochi sits down at the first switchback and looks back at them, in the manner of a guide waiting for slow clients.)
Switchbacks. Cut switchbacks, Lake — that’s a month of masons per turn. Somebody needed to get down there very badly, a very long time ago.
Down where? All I can see is gorge.

beats.title: The valley from above
beats.summary: The gorge in one breath: river, five locks, a painted town down both walls — a whole country not on the sheet.

blocks.context: cutscene — the valley pan, spliced inside the open (playValley)
lines: They stepped out of the trees onto the rim of the world, and the other thing was this:
The gorge was FULL. The river came in silver from the south and stepped down five great timber stairs, and a town went down with it.
Houses stacked down both cliffs, painted every colour a boat can be. Strings of unlit lanterns crossed the air like beads on a wire.
Not on the sheet. A whole town, Lake. Not on the sheet.
Read it off the water. In high on the south. Five locks — that’s a STAIR, for boats.
Town on both walls, where the work is. And out the far end, low and easy — north, into the haze.
That hairline in the right-hand cliff will be a spillway. The rest is people.
The little lights, strung straight across the air. Bridges?
Lantern-strings, cliff to cliff. They tie the town’s two halves together and light the knot at night. …I like them already.
(Mochi regards the town with the enthusiasm of a cat regarding a very large wet staircase. There had better be fish.)
Smoke went up. Gulls came down. And out past the last lock the river went on north without waiting for anyone, the way rivers do.

beats.title: The chart halt — map-is-wrong
beats.summary: The one impossible thing that finally offends Vesper is bad surveying.

lines: Hold this corner. HOLD it, the wind is a critic.
My north sheet. Forty years old, surveyed by a man with a theodolite and a reputation.
It shows this road running on through open heath. Flat. Six more miles of confident little grass symbols.
The road is going DOWNSTAIRS. There’s a gorge here you could lose a cathedral in, and a river at the bottom — I can hear the river.
And my best chart of this whole country says: heath.
Maybe the theodolite man never came this far.
Oh, he came. He got tired, or the light went — and he guessed, and inked the guess like a survey.
The oldest sin in cartography. Forty years of travelers have carried it since.
(Pen. Rule of the trade: the map is corrected the day you catch it, or the lie outlives its maker.)
(Eleven days of impossible things, and the one that finally offends her is bad surveying. Good to know what a person’s for.)
An iron bracket bolted to the rock, empty, at lamp height. Whoever took the lamp took the bolts too. Thrift, or reverence.

blocks.context: the corrected sheet, after the beat (flavor)
lines: The north sheet, corrected in the field: one gorge, one river, one town, inked over forty years of confident heath. The annotation reads "SURVEYED, this time. —V."
Down the switchbacks — follow the road
Down — Dellhollow is not on the map

beats.title: The Stranger across the ravine
beats.summary: A figure on the far rim road bows — low, at something carried — and Mochi makes a sound he has never made.

lines: Across the ravine — a stone’s throw away, and an hour’s walk, and no way over — the old rim road ran on north. Somebody was standing on it.
(A sound is coming out of Mochi that neither of them has ever heard a cat make. Low. Level. Aimed across the gap.)
(Tall. Hooded. Standing the way a post stands. Something at his side keeps catching the light. Glass?)
Hello the road! Is there a crossing north?
The figure did not answer, and did not wave. It turned to face them across the ravine — and bowed.
Deep, and slow, and formal: a bow with rules in it. Aimed low — at something carried, not at anyone carrying it.
Between one blink and the next, the rim road was empty.
Gone. There is no cover on that stretch. I am LOOKING at the absence of cover.
He bowed. That was a real bow — a taught one. Grandmother had one like it and I never learned what it was for.
And he aimed it low. At my hands. At the—
Don’t finish that sentence, I’m not ready to file it.
(Entry: one figure, far rim, hooded. Conduct: courteous. Departure: unexplained. Cat’s opinion: extensive, recorded in full.)
First time in his life he’s made that sound. I’d have been happy never learning he could.

beats.title: The vista
beats.summary: The second look, from the parapet: the town stops being geography and starts being NOISE.

lines: The last switchback turned them around a shoulder of rock, and the gorge opened below like a lit window.
Closer now, the town stopped being geography and started being NOISE. Hammers. Gulls. Somebody laughing, somebody selling something. And under everything, the river working.
I am listening to it. …I’d forgotten what a Tuesday sounds like.
(Two days since the square went quiet. My ears have been ringing with it the whole way.)
(And down there it’s just… going on. All of it. Going on.)
The parapet’s top is polished smooth. Four hundred years of people have leaned here to look at home.
Back up to the Gate? No. The rounds only go one way now.
Not yet. My sheet says this gorge doesn’t exist. I’m correcting that first.
Up the switchbacks at THIS hour? Nothing up there but weather. Everything worth anything is down.
The deep stairs are lock business. Nobody walks them without my say — or my daughter.

beats.title: Arrival — the stair-street
beats.summary: Tar, bread, wet rope, roasting chestnuts — and nobody stares. The town happens to them from every side at once.

lines: Dellhollow, of the five locks. It smelled of tar, bread, wet rope and roasting chestnuts, and it sounded like everything Emberbrook had stopped being.
The road became a street, and the street became a stair. Houses stood on each other’s shoulders, every door painted in somebody’s leftover hull-colours.
Washing overhead. Bunting from some long-finished regatta nobody ever took down. The whole loud town descending, arguing, to the water.
Nobody stared. A woman in a bread-window quoted them a price on principle. Two children ran through the party without slowing. It was wonderful.
A stair with SHOPS on it. A bread-window. A public cistern with a polished cup.
Lake — people. Doing ordinary things, at volume, on top of each other, on a cliff.
(No pedestal. No keeping-flame. I’ve read every doorway on the way down — just oil lamps on strings, lit by whoever’s nearest, meaning nothing.)
(And it holds. It’s loud, and it’s kind, and there’s no flame at all.)
(…Grandmother, what else didn’t you tell me? Or what didn’t you know?)
(A footbridge creaks overhead: a woman crosses with a basket of eels on her hip, treating the air between the cliffs as a footpath.)
(Mochi has caught wind of the eel-stall, somewhere below. The party’s marching order has quietly changed.)
Quay first — you read a town from its edges. Then whoever’s in charge.

blocks.context: talking to Sorrel at the bread-window, first time
lines: Mind the drip-line, loves — wash overhead, bread underhand. Half-loaf’s a penny, whole loaf’s a penny and a look at your cat.
Paid in full. Here’s the heel for him — don’t tell the gulls.
Nineteen days of stuck boats is nineteen days of boat-folk buying bread. Best worst thing ever to happen to this town.
My ovens haven’t cooled since. Don’t tell the harbormistress which way I’m praying.
Half-loaf’s still a penny. The cat’s credit is good.

blocks.context: talking to Old Creel on his step, first time
lines: Four hundred years of stairs, stranger. The town’s knees give out before the timber does.
I’ve spliced rope on this step since I was the boy with the gulls — and there’s always a boy with the gulls.
The bridges are sound. I splice what they hang from — I’d know first.
Rope tells you before it goes. So do most things, if you’re the sort that listens.
Mind your feet going down. Coming up, mind everything else.

blocks.context: talking to Nib, the gull officer, first time
