lines: That one’s Bailiff, that one’s Soup, and the big one’s called the Harbormistress — don’t TELL the harbormistress.
She knows anyway. She knows everything.
Are you the flame people? You’re littler than the quay said.
Soup! SOUP! …He knows his name. He just doesn’t respect it.
The public cistern, fed off a bypass race somewhere above. A tin cup on a chain, worn bright by four hundred years of the same thirst.
Laundry strung wall to wall, three stories up, snapping in the gorge wind. The town flies its ordinary flags daily, and nobody salutes.

blocks.context: the deck chalk and its rude gull (flavor)
lines: Chalked on the deck boards: load-tallies, initials, somebody’s sums — and a rude but accurate drawing of a gull. The gulls on the rails above have declined to comment.

beats.title: The quay
beats.summary: Down the stairs to the water: the quay reads the strangers before the harbormistress does.

blocks.context: talking to Captain Hobb, first time (nineteen days of pumpkins)
lines: Don’t buy anything, don’t lean on anything. Come to gawp at the eel? Gawp at forty ton of pumpkins instead.
Going SOFT, the lot of them. In elegant rows.
How long have you been in the queue?
Nineteen days. “Cut them for pie,” my wife says. Nineteen days of my wife saying pie.
It was a bulk contract, madam. Harvest-fair, downriver. There is no fair for a November pumpkin.
Don’t be sorry, be useful— no. No, forgive me. Nobody’s useful against the river. First thing you learn up here, last thing you believe.
You want north, I hear. So do forty ton of pumpkins. Get in the queue — it’s a very patient queue. We’ve named the seagulls.

blocks.context: talking to Watchman Pell, first time (the pale-blue light)
lines: Watchman. Night shift. It is presently day, which is why I’m holding a wick-knife and a grudge.
Every wick, every noon, so they’ll burn every night. No ceremony to it, friend — oil goes in, light comes out.
And I’d thank the town to remember who carries the ladder.
Odd stretch of nights, mind. Two nights back, on the rim walk: a light going north along the old high road. Steady.
Didn’t bob, the way a carried lantern bobs. And pale — pale BLUE, like the heart of a hail-cloud.
Put my own lamp up, which is the whole language I’ve got. It stopped. A long stop.
Then it went on north, and I found I’d sat down on the wall without deciding to.
Marsh-gas, the harbormistress says. Aye. Well. Marsh-gas doesn’t stop to look back at you.
Sleep’s for the day shift. Which is now. Which is the grudge.
(Mochi is sitting at the eel-stall with the composure of a paying customer. The eel-wife has already fed him twice. Neither of them has admitted it.)
Boats lashed hull to hull, three deep, gangplanked into a floating lane. Washing between the masts, herbs in a bailing bucket. The queue has become a neighborhood.
Forty tons of pumpkins in elegant rows. The nearest rank has quietly begun to slump. Captain Hobb has turned the worst of them to face away from the quay.
Smoked eel by the yard. The eel-wife’s sign reads "FRESH — ASK HER YOURSELF." The quay finds this funnier than visitors do.
RULINGS OF THE HARBOR. One: the river is right. Two: in disputes, see Ruling One. Three: no boat works Lock Five while the Tenant is below. — O.
The bypass races still turn the town’s wheels — grinding, sawing, hoisting. The river is only shut to things that float.
A lamp-pole, a ladder, and a wick-knife on a string. In a flame village this corner would be a shrine. Here it is a chore, and the town sleeps just as sound.

blocks.context: the keepers’ stilt-house door (flavor, before/after supper)
lines: Pulled to. The lamp inside is banked low. Let the house keep its keeper tonight.
The keepers’ door, up on its trestles, the lock tailwater running black under the floorboards. Every other house in Dellhollow stands on the town. This one stands on the job.
The lockhead — ask the harbormistress about passage north

beats.title: The jam — Odessa’s ruling
beats.summary: The river is the road, and the road is shut: the Tenant lies on the only water out of the gorge, and the town is polite to its river.

blocks.context: talking to Odessa before hearing the quay out
lines: Walk the quay before you spend my time, strangers. The town will tell you most of what I would — and shorter.

blocks.context: cutscene — the ruling at the lockhead (playJam)
lines: Harbormistress. You’ll be the pair off the rim road — the quay’s told me twice already, with improvements.
Say your business plain. I’ve a town of idle boats to keep from stupidity.
Vesper — mapmaker. Lake — lamplighter. We need to go north, faster than walking. Everyone we’ve met says the river is the road.
The river IS the road. The road is shut.
Lamplighter, you said. Off the rim road. …Emberbrook, then. The flame-village on the high valley. You’re a long way below your lamps, boy.
Yes. And you’ll hear it crooked off a fish-cart soon enough, so I’ll say it straight.
Two nights ago our flame was taken. All of it, in a breath. The village stands — fed, housed, safe.
And every soul in it has gone flat. They know their own lives like a ledger, and can’t feel one line of them.
We’re going north to bring the flame home.
…I’ve heard of your lamps the way you’ve maybe heard of our floods. Neighbors’ weather.
Took the— the LIGHTS? All the lights at once? Who’s minding the ovens? A village can’t just— somebody has to mind the ovens.
I’m only saying. Terrible thing. Terrible. My cousins downriver won’t believe half of it.
(They’re sorry the way you’re sorry for an earthquake across the sea. Real sorrow, with nowhere in them to land.)
Then you have my sympathy, and my sympathy moves no water. Come to the beam. I’ll show you what shut my road.
Five locks step this water down to the low country. Nineteen days ago, something moved into Lock Five and shut it better than gates ever did.
An eel — river-eel, the old kind. Long as a grain-barge, and lying on the only water out of this gorge.
And you can’t… move her along? Drive her down?
Mind how you talk about her in my town.
This town is not frightened of an eel — carry that upriver and down, with my compliments.
This town is POLITE to this river. Politeness has kept Dellhollow standing four hundred years. Towns that argued with the river are gone.
What she said. It isn’t fear. It’s manners.
And nobody who’s seen her close is in a hurry to be impolite. Which is a different thing from the other word. Which nobody has said.
My ruling stands as posted. No boat works Lock Five while she’s below.
She came up for her own reasons; she’ll go down for her own reasons. The river asks a season — the town waits a season.
We can’t spend a season. Truly. Every day we’re slow, home gets flatter.
Then walk the high road, or wait with the pumpkins. Those are the choices I’ve got to sell.
The high road crossed a ravine this morning without us — there’s a gap in it you could post letters down. How far north does it actually get?
To the Falls Span. Which fell sixty years before I was born — a yard of it left on each rim.
Everything north of here goes by water. That is what Dellhollow is FOR — and just now, Dellhollow isn’t going either.
(There’s my heath explained. The sheet was drawn while the span still stood — the road ran on, so the pen ran on.)
I’m sorry for your village, lamplighter. I am. But I won’t drown polite strangers to save it faster, and I won’t—
My ruling stands as posted. The deep stairs are open to you — my daughter will show you. The stairs, mind. Not the water.

beats.title: Maren, entering wet
beats.summary: The harbormistress’s daughter is fished out of Lock Five for the tenth time, and the tally beam says the why out loud.

blocks.context: cutscene — mother and daughter at the beam (playMarenWet)
lines: Fished this out of Five. Again. Swimming, if you please. In the dark. In November. Over THAT.
Under. “Over” implies I stayed on top. Morning, Ma.
Before you start — she watched me the whole way down and the whole way up, and she did not care.
Nine dives now, and she’s never so much as turned that eye—
The point does not stand. The point sinks, like everything else you put in that lock. Home. Dry clothes. Now.
Can’t. Company. …Hello! You’re the flame people — whole quay says. Is the cat part of it? The cat looks official.
Then before my mother says what she’s going to say: take me on. I’m the best water-eye on this quay — every captain rafted out there knows it.
I’ve crewed these locks since I was five. I can read this river the way your mapmaker reads a— a map. I know what’s in Five better than any soul living.
And I’m seventeen, which is grown. Ask anyone who isn’t my mother.
I’ve heard every word of it since you were six and rowing the wash-tub. The answer is the answer. No child of mine works the north river.
Say why. Out loud. In front of strangers, say the why.
…Mind the beam when you shout. You’ll smudge your father’s marks.
(On the old balance beam, low down: chalk tallies gone grey under wax. A big hand’s work, ended mid-row.)
(Above them, climbing every year, fresher marks in charcoal — a smaller hand’s. Nobody has ever cleaned this beam.)
That’s not an answer. It’s never been an answer.
It’s the whole answer. He was the best eye this river ever grew — better than you, and you’re better than everyone alive.
The north water took him anyway. Between one heartbeat and the next, on a fair morning, in June.
I hauled his boat back up the portage myself. I tar it every winter. I’ve never once let myself ask why.
And I will not stand on my own quay and watch it go north again with you in it.
Dry clothes. Then — since you’re the standing authority on Lock Five — take our guests down and show them what’s shut my river.
SHOW, Maren. The stairs. Not the water.
…She tars the boat. Every winter. She thinks I don’t know.
(New page. “Dellhollow: alive, loud, and not saying the word afraid.”)
(Her ledger is charcoal on her own arm. The beam is a ledger too.)

blocks.context: talking to Maren at the stairhead
lines: Deep stairs, then. Ma said show you, so I’m showing you — try to look shown when we get down there.

The old balance beam. Low down, under wax: grey chalk tallies in a big hand, ended mid-row. Above them, in charcoal, a smaller hand’s — renewed every morning. Nobody has ever cleaned this beam. Nobody ever will.
Down to Lock Five — the stairs, not the water

beats.title: Lock Five — the Tenant
beats.summary: A cathedral that works for a living, the oldest thing in the river — and a plan that needs water, a boat, and a pilot.

lines: The stairs went down into the cool black under the town — two hundred wet timber steps, the river talking to itself inside the walls.
Mind the forty-first step, it lies. So — Lake of Emberbrook. Vesper of— what’s yours? Everyone’s of somewhere.
I’m of HERE. Four generations — you can’t get rid of us with a flood. They tried.
Emberbrook. Born a lane off the square. I know every window.
No. It isn’t. Mind your forty-first step.
(loud whisper, to Lake) Is she always—
The marks on your arm. You’re a ledger too.
Locks worked. Da chalked his on the beam; I wasn’t allowed the beam yet, so — arm. Charcoal washes off, so I redraw them every morning.
Lock Five was a cathedral that worked for a living: black timber out of lantern-reach, chains like bell-ropes, and a flooded chamber of still, dark water.
(She is there. She was always going to be there, and it still lands like a hand closing on the back of the neck.)
(A body thicker than a barrel, moss-and-bronze, old scars like map-lines, laid in two easy coils. One pale eye, clouded like a lamp behind fog, is open.)
Ten times. Look at her. LOOK at her. She was in this river when the locks were still trees.
(The eye moved. Not at the lantern — at us. I have never been read so thoroughly by anything, and I grew up under my grandmother.)
The Tenant, the quay calls her. Under Dellhollow longer than any family in it. Never once paid rent.
Now the part nobody up top will hear me out on. She’s not hunting, and she’s not lost.
Eels her size hold the deep banks, the low country. They do not climb five locks for fun.
And watch. Every hour, near enough, she does THAT.
(The coils unwind by a fathom. She crosses to a sealed grate in the left wall and lies against it, whiskered chin to the bars.)
(A long moment. Then she comes back, and settles, and watches them again.)
The grate. What’s behind the grate?
Sluice gallery. Old workings — draws the chamber down when they need her dry. Sealed since granddad crewed.
She’s not resting against it. She’s TENDING it. Circuit, wall, back — that’s not an animal loafing. That’s a round.
The weed. There’s weed packed through those bars — I saw it on dive six and called it flood-trash. She CARRIED it there.
She’s nesting. Eggs in my sluice gallery — that’s why she won’t go down—
She won’t go anywhere. You don’t walk away from a nest.
(quiet) Funny, though. The old pilots always said the deep banks were thick with her kind.
It’s been dead quiet down there this year. Maybe that’s why she came all the way up.
So it’s worse than the town thinks. Eel eggs, in cold water? She could be over that gallery till spring.
Ma’s season just got five months longer, and nobody knows it but the four of us.
Then nobody waits her out, and nobody in their right mind moves her off a nest. Which leaves— Maren. What is that?
(High in the cliff wall: a round timber-ringed mouth, dry and dark, big enough to swallow a boat whole.)
(Two winches crouch on the apron before it, under a hundred years of grease and rust.)
The flume. High-water spillway — the old boys cut it to shoot timber past the bottom locks, straight down to the tailwater pool.
Dry since before I was born. A mile of black, drops like a stair, and the head-gate winches seized when granddad was young.
But it goes DOWN. Past the locks. Past her — without opening one gate over that nest.
It goes down. …You’d need water in it — the head-gates draw off the top of this pool. She’d feel weather, nothing worse.
You’d need a boat that can take a beating. And you’d need a pilot who holds the mile of black in her head.
Said show you Five. I’m showing you Five.
(For the record: the plan is insane, the pilot is seventeen, and the chart of the flume exists in exactly one living head. …I’ve been that head. New page.)

blocks.context: talking to Maren after the plan is made
lines: The flume goes DOWN. Past her, past the locks. It wants water, a boat, and a pilot. Tell my mother none of that, in any order.
Still black water. She is watching. She was watching before you looked, and she will be watching after you stop.
The sealed gallery, dark weed packed through its bars strand by strand. Her eggs are behind that door, and she is lying guard. …Move along quietly.
A timber-and-iron grate, low over the water in the far wall, dark weed packed through its bars. Sealed workings — lock business, older than anyone doing it.
A mile of black, dropping like a stair through the inside of a cliff. Boats were never the flume’s business. There is a first time for everything, ideally with a pilot.
A century of grease gone to amber. The left drum might turn, with conviction. The right one has become geology.
Clinker-built, tar-dark, rope fenders, a lantern hook at the prow. The tar is this winter’s. Somebody does this boat’s rounds, and has never once said so.
High in the chains hangs a shrouded shape, small and boat-sized, hoisted clear of the flood. The knots are renewed. Somebody tends whatever sleeps up there.
Evening — back up to the quay

beats.title: Supper at the keepers’ cottage
beats.summary: Odessa hosts by serving; Maren talks too much; one locked drawer sits in the middle of the table, politely orbited — and night falls with the step joke.

blocks.context: glue — the supper call at dusk (playSupperCall)
lines: They came up out of the lock-dark into the last of the light. Dusk slid down both cliffs, and the first lantern went up the stair-street.
There you are. Right — orders, and not mine, so don’t argue with ME: Ma says the flame people eat at ours tonight.
She SAYS. Asking is for the guild.
It’s the house over the locks — up on the trestles. You can’t miss it; the river runs right under the floor.
I’ll go ahead. Somebody has to warn the stew.
Yes, the cat’s invited. The cat was invited FIRST, if you want the order of it.

blocks.context: talking to Maren in the cottage, pre-supper
lines: Door’s open. Lintel’s low — for tall people and opinions.

blocks.context: talking to Odessa in the cottage, pre-supper
lines: Sit or stir, guest. Standing in the middle of a kitchen is for weathervanes.

Small dated marks climb the doorframe: MAREN, and a height; MAREN, and a height — stopping a hand below the lintel, years ago. Her records moved to her arm, and nobody in this house has ever said so out loud.
A man’s oilskin coat on the peg nearest the door, square on its shoulders, oiled this winter. Eleven years of weather have come and gone outside. The coat is ready anyway.
A small drawer in the dresser, under the window. Every latch in this house is worn bright with use. This one keyhole is worn bright with something else. Locked — not stuck. Locked.
Eel-spears, winch-pinions, a gear-puller, a coil of chain: the wall of a house where the river is the family trade. Everything is oiled. Nothing is for show.

blocks.context: the table and the three seats (flavor)
lines: Two chairs, arm-ends worn to shine by four generations of forearms. And a third seat: a stool, the newest wood in the room by eighty years, standing exactly where a chair would. Nobody says why.

blocks.context: the hearth — also the supper trigger (flavor)
lines: The stew-pot on its hook, and a fire kept the way locks are kept: banked exact, nothing wasted, nothing out.
The keepers’ cottage was small, warm, and hard-won. Eel stew on the hook, bread in the window-iron, and the river running quiet under the floorboards.
Wash. Basin’s by the door. The cat eats on the step, and knows it.
(Mochi eats on the step — the WARM half of the step. Odessa pretends not to notice.)
(Odessa serves everyone before herself — in order, without hurry, the way she runs the locks. She still hasn’t sat down.)
So — this is the house! Four generations. That beam’s off a barge that sank in the ’02 flood — Da pulled it out. Grand-da. One of them.
And that’s the good table. We’re eating at the good table. Ma got out the GOOD table.
(Small dated marks climb the doorframe. MAREN, and a height. MAREN, and a height. They stop years ago, a hand below the lintel.)
The doorframe. Emberbrook does the same — Grandmother kept mine on the pantry door.
Ma stopped measuring me at fourteen.
(Odessa doesn’t answer. She refills Maren’s bowl before Maren notices it’s empty. That is the answer.)
Harbormistress — your house has exactly one lock in it. Sorry. I survey rooms; it’s a professional disease.
Every latch in here is worn bright from use. That one keyhole is worn bright from something else.
(A small drawer, in the dresser, under the window. The keyhole is clean, and it doesn’t stick. Nobody at this table believes it’s nothing.)
(A mother, a daughter, and one locked drawer nobody mentions. Filed — and for once, the file stays shut.)
(Lake has found the dish-cloth and the kettle-hook without asking. He and Odessa work around each other like they’ve shared a kitchen for years.)
(From Odessa, that counts as high praise.)
(One peg by the door is already taken: a man’s oilskin coat, freshly oiled, hung square.)
(Lake hangs the cloth on the peg below it, and doesn’t ask. Odessa watches him not ask — and fills his bowl again.)
Anyway — tomorrow we thought we’d see the town. More of the town. There’s… plenty of town.
(She’s the worst liar on this river. Her mother is letting her be.)
Mind the tide, then — while you’re seeing your town.
The stew went, and the bread went after it, and the lamp burned low over the good table.
Night’s down. Take the stair slow, strangers. Mind the—
I taught him the step joke.

They stepped out into full night, the lantern-strings burning across the gorge. Maren had slipped past them somewhere between the bread and the door — two steps at a time, already rigging chains in her head.
Supper at the keepers’ cottage — the house over the locks

beats.title: Night on the quay — the dock
beats.summary: The chapter’s quiet center: lantern-strings over the water, a honeybun changing hands, and Vesper’s whole biography, filed.

lines: Night, on the quay. The lantern-strings burned in long swags over the water — ordinary oil, ordinary light — and under them the town went on with its evening.
Pell lit half of these and complained the whole time. A fish-wife did three while I watched, still arguing about brill.
And it’s enough. That’s the thing rattling around in you tonight, isn’t it. It’s enough.
…I’ll manage. It’s a good rattle. Ask me your real question.
Maren asked where you’re from. You answer everything, Vesper. Usually with footnotes. You didn’t answer her.
…I don’t know where I’m from. That’s the whole answer. It isn’t tragic, so don’t make the face.
We moved when I was six. The way families do — work, weather, roads. No story to it.
My parents are warm people, Lake. Genuinely. Ask about last summer, you get three hours of stories — my father does the voices.
Ask about anything before I was born, you get weather.
“Where did you two meet?” — “Oh, it was a wet spring.” A wet SPRING.
Twenty years of asking, and I’ve got a complete weather record of my own family. Not one placename.
Every family has a fog somewhere. Half of Emberbrook can’t name a great-grandmother.
That’s what I decided too. People move. The past gets left behind for practical reasons.
I remember three things from before. A well with a cracked cap. A fence with a gate that dragged.
And a hill — round, bald on top, off east from a kitchen window.
I remember the hill perfectly. I could draw you the hill — I HAVE drawn the hill. It’s numbered.
And I feel nothing when I do. People feel things about home. It costs you something every time you say “Emberbrook” — I’ve watched it all week.
(She says it like she’s reporting a survey error. Because that’s what she thinks it is.)
And the routes. Eleven years of them. Nobody commissions the routes I walk — I go along everything, in order.
You know what you call walking every road out of every market town on a sheet, in sequence?
A grid. Thank you. A search would be emotional. A grid is just thorough.
Somewhere a well, a fence, and a round bald hill line up out a kitchen window. The day I walk over the right rise, I’ll know it.
And then I’ll have an answer for everyone’s favorite small question.
It’s a filing problem. I file. Don’t make it a wound, or I’ll invoice you for the honeybun I know you’ve been saving.
(Lake hands over the honeybun. He had been saving it. He does not make it a wound.)
For the record — the day you walk over the right rise. I’d like to be there.
(Mochi has been asleep against the woman with no somewhere for the better part of an hour.)
(low) Oi. Flame people. Tide’s slack, town’s asleep, boat’s on the chains. …Well? It’s a very good hour for being impolite quietly.
(One day in, and the girl who can’t leave home is smuggling us out of it. I like her enormously — which is going to be a problem.)
Night shift. The proper one. Every wick burning, and the river behaving. …Go on about your business, friends — quietly.
(Captain Hobb has turned in. The pumpkins keep their own watch.)

blocks.context: talking to Maren after the dock scene
lines: (low) Stairs. Quietly. The town sleeps light and my mother doesn’t sleep at all.
The bench holds the warmth a while after you stand up. That is all it does, and tonight it was enough.
Meet Maren at the deep stairs — quietly

beats.title: The twin winches
beats.summary: The boat comes down out of the dark; the left winch takes two; the widow-winch takes six hands — and the sixth pair arrives unhurried.

blocks.context: cutscene — the winches, and Odessa’s station (playWinches)
lines: Lock Five at midnight was blacker than the flume it kept. The work-lanterns made two small rooms of light in a dark the size of a church.
The Tenant’s eye was open. The Tenant’s eye, they were coming to understand, was always open.
Chains first. She’s watched me rig them all week and offered no opinion — which, from her, is a permit.
(Down out of the dark comes a boat: clinker-built, tar-dark, rope fenders, a lantern hook at the prow.)
(Small, old, and kept. The tar is fresh.)
(I know whose it is before anyone says. Somebody does this boat’s rounds.)
Da’s. Ma thinks it hangs down here because she hauled it here.
It hangs here because I climb down and sit in it, some nights. …Don’t tell her. She has enough weather.
(Mochi looks at the boat. Mochi looks at the water beneath the boat, and at the shape in the water beneath the boat. Mochi sits down to reconsider the terms of his employment.)
Head-gates. Twin winches, twin bars. LEFT winch first, to half — or the flume takes her water sideways and we all learn a great deal very fast.
Both of you on the bar. Don’t stop on the squeal. The squeal is it working.
(A hundred years of rust lets go a degree at a time. Somewhere inside the cliff, the flume mouth begins, hollowly, to breathe.)
HA! Half-gate! Hear her? That’s the flume clearing its throat.
Right winch now — and the right one’s the widow. Seized since granddad. She’ll fight.
(The right-hand bar does not move. It has spent a lifetime becoming part of the cliff, and it declines — politely, completely — to stop being cliff.)
It’s not rust on this one. The drum’s crowned over. We’re two pairs of hands short of—
(There is a lantern coming down the stairs. It does not hurry. It has never needed to hurry. Everyone born in this town knows the harbormistress’s step.)
Forty years I’ve kept this gorge. Did the pack of you imagine a gate-chain moves ANYWHERE in it at midnight without my pillow hearing it?
…That bar is cut for six hands. Move over.
(She pulls the heavy gloves from her belt, worn to the shape of exactly this work, and sets herself at the bar.)
The widow-winch came off her century all at once — and the river stood up and walked into the mountain.
White water took the dry mile in one long roar, and the whole chamber rang like a drum.
FULL GATE! She’s running! Oh, she sounds like Da always said — like weather underground—
The head-gates draw off the top of the pool. Nothing below the waterline feels more than weather — her gallery holds its level.
I cut my teeth on those sums, girl. Don’t look so surprised.
…Who else knows the mile of black.
(It is not permission. It is arithmetic, done out loud. Maren does not whoop. Somehow, she does not whoop.)
Stern for you. Weight low past the eye — all of you, low, and hands inboard.
I’ll take the portage stair. I’ll be at the tailwater before the sun is.
(Boarding order: Maren to the stern like gravity is optional. Lake amidships, the lighter warm inside his coat. Vesper to the bow, notebook triple-wrapped in oilcloth. Mochi—)
(Mochi is not aboard. Mochi is delivering, from the apron, a position paper on boats. Lake holds open the satchel.)
(A long negotiation occurs, at the speed of cat. Mochi boards the satchel facing backward, as if the whole thing were his idea and everyone else were late.)

beats.title: The flume run
beats.summary: The held-breath glide past the watching eye, and then a mile of roaring black with Maren calling the timings.

lines: Maren pushed off with one long stroke, and the pool took them: black water, dead quiet, the lantern-strings of Dellhollow a hundred feet up like somebody else’s stars.
And then the water to starboard was not water.
(She has risen. Not at them — beside them: a wall of moss-and-bronze sliding past at arm’s reach.)
(The one pale eye, huge and calm, level with the gunwale. Watching.)
(whisper) Oars in. Weight low. Nobody row — we’re guests.
(The lighter is warm through my coat, and the eye finds it — and rests there. The way old women look at other people’s grandchildren.)
(The eye goes over each of us in turn, unhurried. …Approved. Apparently. Filed.)
For the length of three boats, the oldest thing in the river looked at them, and they let themselves be looked at.
Then she sank away under the black, back toward her sealed door and everything she was keeping behind it.
BOW LEFT! Left, left— good. First drop in three— two— hang on—
(The bottom falls out of the world. The prow lantern draws one gold line down a mile of roaring black.)
Second drop’s a dog-leg — fend RIGHT— Da called it three lengths, it’s two now, the wall’s slumped — fend—
(A wave the temperature of January comes over the bow. Vesper shields the notebook with her entire body. Priorities.)
(Light: held. Cat: yowling. Pilot: laughing. Grandmother, the round has gotten strange — and I am sorry to report I’m having the time of my life.)
LAST GATE! It’s open— it was never even shut proper—
She oiled it. Ma, you absolute— she OILED the tail-gate—
And then: sky. Stars, actual stars, wheeling to a stop overhead.
The flume spat them long and flat across the tailwater pool below the last lock, and the roar fell away behind them.
(Silence. Steam off the water. Far above, very faint, the lantern-strings of Dellhollow. Mochi emerges from the satchel and begins, immediately and furiously, to wash.)
…Under four minutes. Da’s best was six.
(quiet) Beat your time, Da. You’d have hated that. …You’d have loved that.

beats.title: The landing — Maren joins
beats.summary: Dawn at the tailwater: the bag, the boat, and a dead pilot’s wrong chart set in the right hands.

lines: They warped the boat in to the old stone landing, and bailed, and wrung, and were loudly alive at one another, until the sky went pearl.
Odessa was on the landing before the sun was. Of course she was. She had her lantern, a rope’s-end coiled over one shoulder — and a bag.
Packed a week ago. You were going north the moment that eel gave you an excuse — I’ve watched it come the way I watch weather.
A harbormistress who can’t read her own harbor should hand in the whistle.
A WEEK? You packed it a week ago and you still said no! Nine times you said no!
Ten. And I’d say all ten again. The no was what I owed my own heart. The bag is what I owed yours.
The boat’s yours. It was always going to be yours — I only kept the tar on while it waited.
Fenders are new-roped. Bread in the bow locker, and salt-fish enough to make you sick of salt-fish. Take it.
✦ The party gains the boat — tar-dark, clinker-built, river-worthy
(Mochi walks the gunwale stem to stern, inspecting. The boat appears to have passed. Nobody mentions the satchel.)
(From inside her coat she takes an oilskin tube, worn glossy at the cap from years of handling.)
(She sets it in Vesper’s hands, and keeps her own on it a moment longer.)
(The drawer. The one bright keyhole. She went home in the dark, then — and stood in front of eleven years, and turned the key.)
His chart. The north river — every reach and shoal of it, tailwater to the grey marshes. He drew it from memory, the night before. It’s wrong.
She’ll want to fix it. Don’t let her do that alone.
…I’ve been correcting a wrong map by myself for eleven years, harbormistress. I don’t recommend it to anyone.
Then that’s the whole of my asking.
Ma — I’ll send word. Every town with a fish-queue between here and wherever, I’ll send—
You’ll send charts. A chart is a daughter’s hand on paper. I’ll have the charts.
(There is an embrace. It is brief, and it is total, and Odessa does not care who is watching.)
(aboard, scrubbing her face with her sleeve, absolutely not crying) Right! Stations! Flame people amidships. Cat on the— cat wherever the cat decides. I’m not fighting the cat.
(North, then. By water — the lighter warm against my chest, the old road keeping pace up on the rim.)
(Grandmother — the round’s gotten strange. But I swear it’s still the round.)
(New page. “Party of three, one boat, one cat. One wrong chart to put right.”)
(And a hole in my own sheet, still waiting for its rise. …North.)
✦ North on the river ✦ — the flume behind them, the grey marshes ahead
The current took the little boat the moment it felt her — north, quick and cold, down the water her father drew wrong and her mother let her go to fix.
On the landing, Odessa stood with her lantern until the boat was a speck, and then stood a while longer.
Then she took up her rope and began the long climb home — where a town, a river, and one enormous tenant were waiting, politely, for spring.

beats.title: End card
beats.summary: End of Chapter Two.

chapters.title: Chapter Three — The Lanternstead
chapters.sub: a waystation, a friar, and the first light of the necklace

beats.title: Cold open — the grey road
beats.summary: Off the river at an old stone landing, onto the Order road north — grey, mossed, and measured in lamps.

lines: Two days the river ran them north — then it shoaled grey among the marshes and bent away east, from every road at once.
They put the boat ashore at an old stone landing, moored her the way Maren’s father would have wanted, and walked.
First time she’s ever been moored out of sight of a lock. She’ll keep. I did the knots twice.
The road was Order stone under three hundred years of moss — built by people who measured in lamps, for a walk that stopped.
(Mochi walks exactly down the middle of the road, tail up — the only one of the party who has decided this is a procession.)
There. Dead lamp, ten o’clock, brass door and all. First lamp, partner — as promised.
It’s ours. I mean — it’s the same pattern as ours. Same door, same wick. This whole road belonged to the Order.
Three lamps between here and the rise, and the moths sit thickest exactly where the lamps aren’t. So we do this your way — lamp to lamp.
(A road of my own lamps. Grandmother walked me the village round a thousand times and never once said the round kept going.)
(New page. “The North Road. Surface: Order stone. Weather: grey, permanent. Company: one lamplighter, one pilot, one cat, every moth in the world.”)

beats.title: The dead lamps
beats.summary: Lake lights three dead road-lamps and learns the road was measured in light, not miles.

lines: (One. The door swings like it was oiled last week. Order brass doesn’t rust.)
The moths just… made room. Noted: they don’t cross the lamplight.
A lit lamp is a shut door. Grandmother’s phrase. I never asked who it was shut against.

blocks.context: lighting the second road-lamp — the mile-lamp
lines: (Two. The old walkers measured this road in lamps, not miles. One lamp, one hour.)

blocks.context: lighting the third road-lamp — the street’s taken
lines: (Three. Lit, the road looks kept. Somebody should tell the moths this street’s taken. …I suppose I just did.)
A waymarker, swallowed to the shoulders. The carved hand points north; the mile-count is moss. Vesper records it as “one, presumed.”
The stone hand points up the road. Someone cut a small sun above it — or a lamp. On this road, likely a lamp.
A second waymarker, leaning hard. The carving reads “LANTERNSTEAD —” and then three centuries of weather.
A dark stretch between lamps. The moths sit thickest here, drifting slow, no direction to them.

blocks.context: talking to Mochi on the road

lines: Back to the boat? No. The rounds only go one way now.
That stretch ahead is solid moths. Light the lamps first — nobody walks the dark part of a round.
After dark? Flamebearer, rule one! The road will keep till morning — it has kept three hundred years.
The grey road — light the road-lamps (…/3)…
Make the Lanternstead by dusk — north, lamp to lamp

beats.title: The Stranger on the road
beats.summary: A pale-blue lantern, full to the glass — and a bow to the lighter, not to the men.

lines: Far up the road, where their lamplight ran out, stood a light that was not theirs. A lantern, carried. Full to the glass. And pale, pale blue.
(A sound is coming out of Mochi that none of them has ever heard a cat make. Low. Level. Aimed.)
(Maren — who has stared down the Tenant at arm’s length — takes one look at the far light and steps closer to the others without deciding to.)
(Hooded. Tall. Perfectly still. Nothing about him says resting.)
Sir! The road’s dark past the bend — you’re welcome to walk in our light—
The stranger did not come closer. He looked at them — or at something they carried — for a long moment.
Then he bowed: deep, and slow, and courteous. Not to them. To the small brass flame in Lake’s hand.
Between one blink and the next, the road was empty.
…Gone. Gone HOW? That’s a quarter mile of open road and nothing to be behind.
He bowed. To the lighter — I know where he was looking.
Lamplighters do. On the high days.
Grandmother bowed exactly that deep, exactly that slow. I never saw another soul do it.
(Entry: one walker, unmapped. Lantern: full, blue, wrong. Manner: courteous. Departure: unexplained. Filed under things I refuse to call impossible twice in one week.)
Mochi hissed again. Twice in his life now — both times at that silhouette.
Grandmother used to say: when the cat votes, count it twice.
And how does the cat vote?

beats.title: Arrival — Friar Tally
beats.summary: The waystation is kept, impossibly: the Order’s last friar has the whole liturgy and never had a guest.

lines: They smelled the Lanternstead before they saw it: woodsmoke, turned earth, and — impossibly, out here at the end of the world — laundry.
A tower. A well. A vegetable patch in ruler-straight rows. And… shirts.
…aaaand the ninth observance, the polishing of the glass, la-la-la, that the light find no dust upon arrival—
Oh! Oh. Wait. Wait wait wait — I know this one.
“WHO WALKS the dead road?” — no, sorry, it’s “who KEEPS the dead road,” and then YOU say—
You’re not supposed to ANSWER! Nobody has ever answered!
It’s— yes? It’s my lighter. It’s warm, if you want to—
Don’t— don’t let me touch it. There’s a correct distance. I know the correct distance. I have never once needed the correct distance.
Three hundred years this station has kept the Rite of the Open Door. Firewood dry. Beds aired. Great-lantern polished — I do the glass on Sundays.
You’re real. The office is real. I have the whole liturgy and nobody ever came.
…How long have you been out here alone?
Alone? Madam, I have nineteen crows, a well with opinions, and the entire Order of Lamplighters, bound in thirty-nine volumes.
And now — a Flamebearer and a Waykeeper. A walking two, at my door, at dusk, in the correct season.
The road was never meant to be walked alone. It says so on the door.
There’s three of us, actually. Four — the cat counts.
The rite says two, madam. The rite will simply have to cope.
Vesper. Mapmaker — not a Waykeeper, that I’m aware.
You walked here off the map, madam, in front of a Flamebearer. I won’t argue doctrine with the doctrine standing in my yard.
Emberbrook! The Third Daughter! Founded from a carried ember, one wick, one walking— sorry. I will be doing this all evening.
Tally. Friar Tally — if titles survive being the last one.
You’ll want supper. The rite is clear: the walkers eat first.
Why does everyone on this road make feeding me a LAW?
Because the Order wrote good laws, madam.

blocks.context: talking to Tally, first time (the necklace explained)
lines: Ask me anything! I know everything and have seen none of it. I am the world’s leading authority on things I have never watched happen.
The great-lantern, then. What was it for?
The waystations ring the deep wood — one great-lantern each. Lit, they warded the road for the walkers.
And each answered the next — light in sight of light, all the way around. The rite calls it the necklace.
I also call it the necklace. It’s a good rite.
Since a hundred and nine years before my order bought its last new kettle. We were never a hasty organization.
So — three hundred years, give or take. Noted.

blocks.context: talking to Tally again (the fourteenth keeper)
lines: Friars keep; lighters walk. I’m the fourteenth keeper of this station — and the first to keep it alone.
My teacher taught me the rounds the way his teacher taught him: for the day the walking twos came back. He died believing they would.
I feed his crows. And look — he was right.

blocks.context: talking to Tally after that (repeat, on loop)
lines: Eat! Doctrine can wait an hour. Doctrine has waited three hundred years — it’s very good at it.

blocks.context: talking to Mochi at the Lanternstead
lines: (Mochi has inspected the entire station and is now sitting on the rite-books. Tally appears to regard this as a liturgically significant endorsement.)
Three shirts, patched with liturgical neatness, and one enormous nightcap.
Faded prayer flags, each block-printed with a lamp — the wind says the rite for you while you walk. These have been praying nonstop for three hundred years.
Cabbages in rows straight enough to survey by. A stick label reads “TURNIPS (unconvinced)”.

beats.title: The well
beats.summary: The well was cut by the Order — which is to say, the crank takes two.

blocks.context: Tally, if only one keeper is at the well
lines: The crank takes two, friend — that is not a metaphor, it is engineering.
Supper wants water, and the Order cut this well — the crank takes two. Everything here takes two; it was that kind of Order.
(The bucket arrives. It contains water, and one entirely unhurried frog.)
That’s Brother Frog. He predates me. Back he goes.
The well. Somewhere down there, Brother Frog continues his ministry.
Help Tally draw water — the well takes two

beats.title: Supper
beats.summary: The rite pays off at the table: four places laid every night, and at last the arithmetic comes out.

blocks.context: cutscene — supper at the round table (playSupper)
lines: The round room at evening: candle-brass and cook-fire, thirty-nine books, and a big table laid — plates, spoons, and a jug of flowers that on inspection were mostly kale.
Sit! Sit. The rite is clear—
…the walkers eat first. Yes. I’m coming to terms with a road where every law is about dinner.
The good laws usually are, madam.
(Supper is bread, butter, and turnip-and-barley out of the big pot — Sister Kettle. The walking kettle is her novice.)
(The table is set for four. It has very plainly been set for four for a long time.)
You lay four places. Every night?
Every night, madam. One for the keeper, two for the walking two, one for whoever the road sends extra.
The arithmetic has never once come out before. Forgive me if I keep counting you.
Four places, five of us — four, and a cat who counts. …I’ll get the wobbly stool. I ALWAYS get the wobbly stool.
Alone it’s the same pot — turnips, barley, whatever the garden forgives me. The art is making Tuesday taste different from Wednesday. Wednesdays, onions. It is a whole liturgy.
(Tally ladles the top of the stew onto the fourth plate and sets it down for Mochi. Mochi inspects the plate, then Tally — and approves the shipment.)
High praise. The crows only ever shout.
Now — supper’s price. A walker pays in news; that’s station law.
Tell me one true thing about Emberbrook. Not the founding — I HAVE the founding. A small thing, the kind no book keeps.
Poppy — our baker. She burns her thumb on the first tray every morning, and swears tomorrow she won’t.
She’s sworn it every morning of my life.
…A baker, swearing at the bread, daily, on schedule. Thirty-nine volumes on that shelf and not one of them thought that worth writing down. I shall begin the fortieth.
…When did we stop being strangers? I’m asking honestly. I can’t find the entry.
There wouldn’t be one. Somewhere between Poppy’s buns and Odessa’s stew, I’d say.
(Two towns, one river, one cat. On paper we hardly know each other. Noted, for the file: the paper is wrong.)
The candles burned down a knuckle. Outside the little window, the last of the grey went out of the sky.
(Tally rises and unhooks a tiny brass bell from his belt. He rings it once — a sound the size of a teaspoon.)
The walker’s grace. Forgive the ceremony — I have never once got past the first line with anyone but the crows.
“Light on the road behind you; light on the road ahead. Eat and be kept. Walk and be expected.”
Thirty-nine hand-copied volumes, worn soft with re-shelving. Volume One falls open to the creed: “Light does not die—”. The rest of the page is worn away by a thumb.

blocks.context: the round room — the hearth and the empty bracket (flavor)
lines: The hearth holds a small, careful cook’s fire. Above the mantel: an empty bracket, polished, exactly the size of a lamplighter’s lighter. Waiting.

blocks.context: the round room — the walkers’ bed (flavor)
lines: One bed, made with hospital corners — the walkers’ bed, kept ready three hundred years. One hammock, strung by the window: Tally’s.
Supper at the Lanternstead — go inside

beats.title: Night — the swarm
beats.summary: Rule one, learned the hard way: after dark on the dead road, the lighter is the only lit thing in the world.

blocks.context: cutscene — the moth swarm (playSwarm, first half)
lines: Supper went down to bread-ends and doctrine. Night came down on the Lanternstead like a lid.
(Mochi’s ears go flat. He is facing the courtyard dark. He is very, very still.)
The cat. The books have PICTURES of a cat doing that— oh. Oh no. The books say this.
“In the dark season the strays seek the walking flame.” Outside. Everyone outside, NOW.
(Maren has the stance of a woman with a barge-pole and no barge-pole. She puts herself between the swarm and the smallest party member, who is the cat.)
Define “seek the walking flame,” Tally — QUICKLY.
Moths eat light, madam — and your partner is carrying the only lit thing in the world!
Rule ONE: no bare flame outdoors after dark on the dead road. The first rule, and I have never once needed to say it out loud!
They’re coming through my coat— I can’t put it out, it doesn’t GO out—
Moths! — the great-lantern: wick and winch, together

beats.title: The great-lantern
beats.summary: Three hundred years of polish take the flame at last — the necklace gets its first light.

blocks.context: cutscene — wick and winch, together (playSwarm, second half)
lines: Don’t put it out — OUTSHINE it! The great-lantern!
A lit lamp is a shut door; a great lamp is a shut GATE. Wick and winch — it takes two, it always takes two!
Waykeeper — the winch! Nine turns, then HOLD! Flamebearer — the wick-gate. Brass door, same as your lamps, only rather — rather large—
(Mean it. A lamp for the whole road — for every walker who never came, and the one keeper who stayed. …That one’s easy to mean.)
The great-lantern took the flame like a held breath let go.
Three hundred years of polish and readiness — and then LIGHT, out across the grey road in every direction at once.
The swarm broke against it like water on a stone. The moths rose, wheeled once around the tower — and scattered back into the dark, thin and aimless again.
…Ha. Hahaha. It’s— I did the glass on Sundays. Every Sunday. And the books never once say that it’s YELLOW—
(Tally is laughing. Tally is also crying. He does not appear to have noticed either.)
You lit it too, Tally. Whoever keeps the wick dry is lighting the lamp — grandmother’s rule.
You’ve been lighting this one your whole life. It just caught tonight.
…I’m going to write that in the margin of Volume One.
Now. Rule one, said properly this time: no bare flame outdoors after dark.
The stores keep a walking-hood — brass cowl, Order pattern. The flame breathes; the light stays home.
✦ The walking-hood — the lighter travels shielded at night
(Entry: lamps ward moths; great lamps ward roads. After dark, my partner is the most interesting thing in the world to every hungry thing on it. Underlined twice.)
The great-lantern, burning. The courtyard has a heartbeat now. The moths keep to the far dark.
The great-lantern crowns the tower: glass the size of a room, brass polished bright, a wick never once lit in living memory. The cleanest dead thing on the whole road.

beats.title: Morning — the letter
beats.summary: Twenty-Two brings the first letter on the route since Tally’s teacher died — and every line of it is true.

blocks.context: cutscene — the grey post-crow and Rowan’s letter (playLetter)
lines: Morning came up almost blue. Inside the great-lantern’s ring the frost had kept off the vegetable rows — and the crows were shouting about a visitor.
They’re post-crows, madam — the Order’s message line. The route runs to every Heartlight, and the birds still fly it, because I still feed it.
The letters stopped long before me.
She’s carrying. She’s— that is the first letter on this route since my teacher died.
Somebody at our end remembered what the old perch by the gate was for.
It’s his hand. Steady as ever. “To the mapmaker and the lamplighter, gone north.”
“Day two. The village is fed. The mill turns. The weather has been fair for the season.”
“Notices: the baker bakes daily; output normal. The child Pip continues his instruction of the woman Mara; progress is recorded.”
“The fisherman reports that the pond has fish in it. This is all of the news.”
“The ledger is kept. I find I have nothing further to put in this letter, though I sat an hour with it. Provisions follow with the bird. — R. Elder.”
…It’s all true. Every line of it is true, and correct, and in order.
(Rowan makes jokes. Rowan makes jokes the way the mill turns. There is not one joke in this letter.)
He wrote “the woman Mara.” He named her to the flame himself, the day she was born — he told that story every Emberwake.
New page. “Day two: the village is fed, and fading. The facts are keeping. The people aren’t.” …We walk faster.
She used to say a street goes cold slower than it warms. It’s the only mercy we’ve got. Let’s spend it walking.
(Mochi leans, very briefly, against Lake’s boot. Then pretends he didn’t.)
Morning — see what the crow brought

beats.title: The wall-map — Tally joins
beats.summary: The circuit, the count, and a request practiced twice: the keeping goes with the walkers.

blocks.context: talking to Tally before the wall-map (he sends you to the round room)
lines: Before you walk, you should see what road you’re on. The wall-map — in the round room. I’ll meet you at it.
The Order’s wall-map of the circuit: a ring of valleys around the deep wood, one road joining them, a lamp sigil at every name. Under every name, years of tiny meticulous tally-marks.

blocks.context: cutscene — the wall-map and the road to Harrowdel (playWallMap)
lines: Before you walk, you should see what road you’re on.
The circuit. One road, thirteen stations, ringing the deep wood — a Heartlight in every valley.
Every one a daughter of the mother-fire at the middle. Emberbrook, here. The Lanternstead — you are here. First station north.
Dellhollow. We’re ON somebody’s map. Ma’d say that’s no excuse for anything.
And ahead — Harrowdel. Three days up the circuit. A LIVING valley, Flamebearer: their lamps still answer. The last on this road that do.
And the little marks? Under every name?
Sunrises the station has been kept. My teacher started the count; I kept the count.
It’s also where I got my name — the crows had names, and I had marks, so.
It is a great many marks, madam. But the count held. That’s the whole of the friar’s rite: hold the count until the walkers come.
The walker we saw on the road. Pale lantern, full. If he’s out here—
One road is all there is, Flamebearer. Whatever he keeps, he keeps it somewhere ahead of you.
(Mochi is sitting in the doorway, facing north.)
Which brings me to a request I have practiced, so let me get it out.
The station kept the road FOR the walkers. The walkers are back. So the keeping goes with you — that’s doctrine. I checked. I checked twice.
Tally. Are you asking to come with us?
The road was meant to be walked by two. …Nobody ever said ONLY two.
I’ll fetch the kettle. Not the good kettle. The WALKING kettle. We have DOCTRINE about kettles—
(For the record: party of four, one cat, one crow flying the route behind us. The chart is getting crowded. I find I don’t mind.)
Twenty-Two flies the circuit — she’ll find us wherever the road puts us. The rest stay and keep the necklace. Somebody must.
✦ The road to Harrowdel ✦ — three days north — the last living valley on the circuit
They left the Lanternstead burning behind them — the first light of the necklace, lit again; a shut gate at their backs and three days of grey road ahead.
Behind them, the great-lantern held its one note of yellow against the winter, saying what the Order always meant it to say: keep walking. You are expected.
The round room — ask Tally about the road ahead

beats.title: End card
beats.summary: End of Chapter Three.

lines: the necklace has its first light

chapters.title: Chapters 4–10 (planned)
chapters.sub: the arc of the Long Rekindling — no dialogue written yet

beats.title: Ch. 4 — Harrowdel
beats.summary: One of the last living valleys, its elderly keeper failing; the Warden recalls Harrowdel before the party’s eyes — first face-to-face, first bow. Sable the heretic moth-catcher joins in cold fury; Marrow the ferryman — payment only in memories — is met en route, feels one for the first time in years, and poles after them: “You’ve paid me before. Long ago.”

beats.title: Ch. 5 — Ashfield
beats.summary: Intact, grey, silent — found off Maren’s father’s wrong chart. The double reveal: this happened before, and this is Emberbrook’s future if they fail. Vesper’s birthplace; her parents’ fog explained and exonerated in the same stroke; Lake finally says grandmother’s sayings as teaching, not grief.

beats.title: Ch. 6 — The Parley
beats.summary: The Warden speaks: the Kindling is dying; he offers Lake the succession and asks for the lighter. Lake learns of the unclaimed year and takes the knife of it.

beats.title: Ch. 7 — The Mothway
beats.summary: The migration reveals moths as stray moments; a meant fire calls them home; Lake finally understands “mean it” and stops improvising.

beats.title: Ch. 8 — The Gatehouse
beats.summary: The archive of poured valleys. The mechanism of restoration is confirmed — Vesper’s notebook and Rowan’s ledger become the endgame keys; Marrow repays his debt.

beats.title: Ch. 9 — The Pouring
beats.summary: The Kindling is nearly ash; the pour begins; stopping it saves Emberbrook and starves the mother-fire. Nobody is wrong. Cliffhanger on the choice.

beats.title: Ch. 10 — The Long Rekindling
beats.summary: The lighter goes home to the stone bowl; Emberbrook is called back name by name, Pip first; the Warden is kept by the flame he served; the circuit is walked again.
